paragraphs.text: My name is George Nidever. I was born in 1802, Dec. 20, in Sulivan Co., East Tenn. My father, also named George, was a native of Penn; I do not remember the town. His father died while he was quite young. My father removed to Tenn. when about 20 yrs. of age. Here he was married to Christina Punkhouser, a native of Virginia, but whose family had removed to Tenn. some years before. My father's business was farming, which he carried on while he lived. Before & after his marriage he took part, as a volunteer, in the early indian[Indian] wars with the Cherokees, Shawnees, & other [hostile?] tribes. Besides myself, there were [...] 8 other children; three daughters [...] & [...] five (5) sons. I was third; my two brothers John and Jacob being older than I. Of these none but myself now remain. My oldest brother John died here in Santa Barbara about five yrs. ago. Jacob died in Arkansas, do not recollect the year, but think it was about 1848. Mark, who followed [me], was killed in Nov. of 1830, at the beginning of my first hunting expedition. Isabella, who came next to Mark married a man by the name of Harril & died while I was in Arkansas. Henry the next in age, married a Miss Sinclair a native of Illinois; (Mark married into the same family). He (Henry) died in Texas[,] I think about 1850, leaving a large family. Next came Nancy[;] she married, after I left home, to a man by the name of Barker; she died in Arkansas, I think, about 1850. Next in order came Daniel, who left home with a party that went down the Mississippi somewhere about 1837 or 8, & was never heard from. Christina, my youngest sister was drowned in Arkansas.

My family removed, when I was 5 yrs. old, from Sulivan Co., Tenn. to Buncomb[e] Co. N. C. Here we settled down & worked a farm for about 9 years (when I was nearly 14 yrs. of age), when we went to Crawford Co., Mo. & settled on a farm situated on a river called the Moro. Here we followed farming for several years. When I was about 18, (in 1820 or thereabouts), a party of 7 families started through the wilderness for Arkansas, & my brother Jacob & I accompanied them, with a few cattle we took to sell, for the purpose of seeing Arkansas, & with the intention of going back for our family, if we found the country good.

Among our party there were about twenty men, including my brother & myself, all frontiersmen & a sufficient number to insure protection from the indian [Indian] tribes we might encounter. We had no adventure of note during our journey of over two months although we saw many parties of indians [sic] & passed through the country of the Osage Indians. They stole a few of our horses but his did not offer to trouble us in any other way.

About half way on our journey our bread gave out & we were obliged to live on meat the rest of the way. The whole country through which we passed was teaming filled with game, so that we never wanted for fresh meat. We had left Mo. in the fall, about the month of Oct. & in December we reached Ark.

Our point of destination was the Six Bull river, north of the Arkansas, a locality formerly visited by some members of our party, & where they had decided to make their homes. Two or three months later, however, they were obliged to move south of the Arkansas, owing to this section having been ceded by the Govt. to the Cherokee & other Indians. They finally settled near or in the vicinity of Fort Smith. Among these families there were two or three by the name of Mathers, one Blevin, & one Harril, the same family into which my sister afterwards married.

My brother Jacob sold his stock, consisting of hogs & cattle, to the Choctaw & Cherokee Indians, at a good bargain, & settled down on a farm just below Fort Smith. The next year all of our family came from Mo. & settled a few miles from my brother Jacob's place.

[...]Soon after our party left the Six Bull river for the neighborhood of Fort Smith, I left them for Texas in company with a young man by the name of Daniel Shipman, one of our party from Mo., who, like my self, fond of travel and adventure, & with a desire to see that state, had determined to make a trip through that section of the country. We went to that this portion of Texas then known as Austin's Grant, visited several parts of this tract & two months later returned to Fort Smith. Shipman soon returned to Texas with his father & mother & several brothers & sisters who had come from Mo[.] with us. They begged me to go with them but I had no desire to return at that time.

From 1822 until 1828 18[??] I made my home with my family. In 1827, I think, my mother died. In 1828 Alex. Sinclair, a young man living near Fort Smith, and I went up the Canadian fork of the Arkansas & began the building of a large raft of cedar logs, which we intended to float down to New Orleans & sell. We were engaged, with a few men we hired, nearly a year in fmaking [making] this raft. When everything was ready & we were about to start down the river the Cherokees to whom that section had been ceded, attempted to seize our raft, but taking advantage of a freshet we sailed down in the night beyond their reach, & escaped them. This availed us but little however for at the mouth of the fork, the raft ran aground[,] was broken up, & we abandoned it.

This misfortune decided both Sinclair & myself to take to hunting & trapping, so in May of 1830 we joined a party of trappers & hunters that was forming just above Fort Smith. [...] A man by the name of Bean, commonly called Colonel, a native of Tenn. I think, or at least a former resident of that state although he had been living for some time in Ark. was at its head. He was an elderly man & by occupation a Gunsmith. It was thought He had never had any experience in hunting & trapping like that which was before us &, in fact, of our whole party of 48 men but three had fought the indians [sic] & none had ever trapped.

In those days the beaver were[,] it was supposed[,] plenty in the streams of the Rocky Mountains & the trappging of them, although attended with great risks owing to the indians [sic], is was largely engaged in by the frontier settlers. Col. Bean was the first to propose the forming of a hunting & trapping expedition; & he had no trouble in raising from among the families in the vicinity our party of 48. Most All of our men could handle the rifle & like myself had been broughts [sic] up to the hunting of small game, although I had already, as some three others, become [...] a good deer & buffalo hunter. Before I was 9 yrs. old I began to use fire arms, & being very fond of hunting & shooting at a target I soon became an excellent shot. When I went with the party from Mo. I had already become one of the crack shots at the annual shooting matches in the section where I lived, having but very few equals. This of course encouraged me to greater effort, & with the continual practice the then abundance of game then afforded me, I soon became one of the most successful hunters in that portion of the country. Among our party from Mo. there were three of the best hunters detailed to supply the Camp with game meat, but notwithstanding they were good shots[.] I could always kill three times as much game as any of them, so that ere long I was appointed the chief hunter of the party. This piqued the old hunters not a little, who attempted to attribute the excellence of my shooting to my rifle, but, having exchanged guns with them from time to time, without any apparent change in my shooting, they finally concluded that I could beat them. Our Capt. Harril, had so much confidence in my shooting that he declared I could kill anything if I had only a corncob. I had also in after [sic] years become such a skillful buffalo hunter that on several occasions companies of trappers among the mountains offered to bet as high as $5000 that I could kill more buffalo than any other man in the Rocky Mountains.

Sinclair, my partner in the building of the raft, was a very good shot & buffalo hunter, having been raised on the Western Frontier. He had also done considerable indian [Indian] fighting. He could not travel much on foot, owing to the loss of the toes & part of the left foot, in what manner I do not recollect. He was then about 40 yrs. of age. A younger brother of about 30 also formed one of our party; he was no hunter & but a very ordinary shot.

While in the mountains I was as a general rule detailed to supply the party with fresh meat & as game abounded throughout our whole journey up the Arkansas to the mountains , I was always successful. [...]

Each man was to equipp [equip] himself & hunt for himself, except that we were to keep together in moving about, for mutual protection, & or, as it afterward happened, several men or messes, of six men each, would form a company, dividing equally the skins.

The equippment of each man was rifle & six traps. Besides this, most of the men had a pistol, knife, & a small hatchet that could be conveniently carried in the belt. We had the best of ammunition. For convenience in cooking, &, we were divided into messes of six men each, one of each mess usually volunteering to do the cooking, prefering it to hunting, & he of course received his share of pelts. My equippment [equipment] was the same as that of the others excepting my pistol which I had ordered made. It was the same bore as my rifle, & it would kill a buffalo at 40 yds. Our intention was, when we set out, to be gone about a year or 18 months. We left the vicinity of Fort Smith in May 1830, & followed up the north fork of the Canadian and the Arkansas rivers until we struck the Rocky Mountains, & from there struck across into New Mexico. We had procured a map & with its aid we made our way with little trouble.

When we started we had, each two pack animals & in some cases of us three, so that we travelled slowly, and, besides, frequent freshets kept us back. About the first of July we reached the Cross timbers [sic] about two hundred miles from Fort Smith.

But just here I may as well give you the names of those in our company that I can remember. First came Col. Bean our Captain, whom we had selected in this because he had always borne a good name among us & was very much esteemed rather than from any superior skill or knowledge he possessed in affairs relating to the control & management of such a [an] expedition. Besides being older than most of us. He was accompanied by a son, a young man about 24 yrs. of age. Two men by [the] name of Green, although not related; Basey, a large powerful man; he would probably weigh at least 240 lbs; Carmichael, who was afterwards treacherously killed by a Mexican here in Cal. during one of the revolutions;Allen; [sic] Bowen; Saunders, an old man; Allen, who I think is still living in [L... Cal.,I think] Los Angeles Co.; Baldwin, one of our best shots; Williams; Frazier, who also came to Cal[.]; Weaver who also came to Cal[.]; Graham, of whom I shall have more to say hereafter; Tom Hammonds; Anderson; Huges Hace who left us [...] in New Mexico in 1831 between June & Sept.; Mark my brother; Price; & Potter. These are all the names I can recollect at present, but in the course of my story will no doubt be able to recall those of some of the others.

From the Cross Timbers we continued our course towards the mountains, making very slow marches. About the latter part of Aug. or first of Sept., having marched some 200 to 250 miles since leaving the Cross Timbers,we fell in with a herd of Pawnee indians...that night, six of us started out one afternoon to hunt buffalo. We had been for several days travelling in a country frequented by the Comanches, (principally,) but had seen no signs of them, & had taken no other precautions on the march than to keep close together[,] sometimes riding by twos & sometimes four abreast when the country was open & level. No one was especially detailed to keep a lookout, for the air was so clear that objects could be seen at a very great distance. I frequently saw buffalo as far as 9 mile[s] away, making them out without any difficulty. I have never been in any place since, nor before, where the air seemed so remarkably clear. Then we always rode close together so that within fifteen minutes after an alarm we could have been prepared to receive an enemy. At night, too, ever since entering the indian [Indian] country, we formed our packs into a circular breastwork, with our horses picketed inside, & a guard of from three to five, sometimes more, relieved every four (4) hours, throughout the night. Our party of six, having left the camp, divided separated; three keeping in close to the edge of the belt of timber that ran along the river, the other three, & among them myself, taking the open & higher ground some distance from & opposite the first three. We rode along for some distance in our relative positions, without discovering any buffalo. At last the party skirting the timber saw some horse[s] in the distance, grazing near the edge of the timber, & with the hopes of capturing them, for they naturally thought them wild horses, they approached them under cover. Their surprise as well as alarm, however, can be imagined when, upon nearing the animals the[y] discovered them to be tied. They immediately started at full speed towards us & hardly five minutes after, two Comanche indians [sic] emerged from the timber, on horseback, & rode after our companions. Following these came others until they were, I should judge, about forty altogether. We ran & they folled [followed] us, and, being better mounted than we were, soon came within shooting distance. They discharged a shower of arrows at us, many of them going beyond us. We saw that it was useless to run any farther so [we] stood our ground or [...] rather rode leisurely towards camp. They did not offer to attack us, but one of them, more daring than the rest, charged down on us alone & rode near enough to throw his lance, which was easily dodged. We supposed that he would ride directly by us & for us and as shots are always uncertain as an when fired at an object in such rapid motion we would have let him pass, but instead of doing this he rode off at right angles to our party, in a perfectly straight line, &, loth [loathe] to lose such a chance I drew bead on him & tumblybled him out of his saddle, & some of our boys caught his horse. This brought forth a yell from the indians [sic], who, however, did not trouble us any more. Upon reaching camp we wanted to take half of our men & go back & attack the indians [sic], but Col. Bean refused, to the great dissatisfaction of all of they party who from his ways had already lost some confidence in him & now they suspected, & with reason as will be seen hereafter, that he was not over brave.

At this junction ten (10) of our party left us, declaring their intention to go back to Arkansas, as they feared to go into indian [Indian] country with Bean as Capt. I have never heard what became of them.

In the hopes of avoiding the indians [sic] we decided a day or two after to leave the North Fork & go over to The Arkansas. Our course had lain chiefly along the North bank of the North Fork. The country we had come through was very fine, the land rich, well timbered, especially near the river, & game in abundance. We travelled very leisurely, making on an average from 15 to 20 miles a day striking camp about 7 or 8 o'clock in the morning usually' & pitching camp again all the way from 3 to 5 in the afternoon. During the early part of our journey we were frequently delayed by swollen streams, & occasionally by the illness of some of the men, which would make it necessary to halt a day or two.

As had been decided, we went over to the Arkansas river & having reached it crossed to its Northern side. This river we intended to follow until we should reach the mountains A day or two later, in the early part of the afternoon we came upon several Pawnee warriors, who came out from the ftimber to meet our party, & others joined us as we marched along until they numbered all told about 80. About half of them were mounted. They were a war party as they had no women with them; a few had rifle[s] but the majority had only bows & arrows. They were fine looking indians [sic], about the medium height but well made & active. Their dress was the customary buckskin leggings & the breech clout; a few had shirts. Upon first seeing us they made signs of friendship, but although we considered them friendly indians [sic] we did not place much confidence in their good will. Our leader however was very generous with them, making them presents of blankets, tobacco, & even knifves & powder, which the most of our party very much disapproved of.

It must have been about 2 o'clock in the afternoon when we met the Pawnees, & soon after we went into camp. They went into camp also about 60 yds. from us, in the same body of timber. The next morning we moved on & left them, & the next afternoon camped about the usual hour.

Having to do most of the hunting I usually started out as soon as we got into camp, & generally took my brother Mark with me, while he lived. Sometimes others accompanied me, as a rule either Isaac Graham or my old companion Alex. Sinclair.
This afternoon I started out with Graham, to get buffalo, if possible.

We went fully four miles from camp before we saw anything; then we saw the objects of our search at a distance. We were on foot so had little trouble in approaching them but, before we got with in range I saw an indian's [Indian's] head peeping out of a hollow, & I at once saw that we had been drawn into a trap. I at once told Graham, & we took to our heels at once immediately, with a party of 80 Arapahoes close behind. Two of them only were mounted, but the rest of them were good runners & our chances of saving our scalp[s] was very small. We were out in the open country, the nearest timber being that of the river belt fully a mile & [a] half away, & a pack of fleet footed red skins close upon us. We saw at once that our only hope was to reach the timber where perhaps we might be able to defend ourselves.

Graham was a good runner, the best in fact in our party, & had he wished could soon have left me in the rear, but it was not his character to desert a comrade in danger so we kept together straining every nerve to reach the shelter of the woods. The indians [sic] on horseback of course gained on us at once, & we were obliged to turn alternately & by aiming at them check them for a moment. Under any other circumstances it would have been amusing to see the horsemen make their horses [...] jump quickly from side to side, at the same time throwing their own bodies this way then that to prevent our taking aim on them, when we raised our rifles, but we only realized that each stoppage was enabling those on foot, who were steadily pressing forward, to gain ground. The chase had been kept up nearly a mile, I should judge, when our pursuers were so close upon us that we had determined to fire on them. Graham, although naturally a brave man, was not a little frightened, & was almost exhausted by our long run, so that [...]in his fright he did not see the foremost indians [sic] throw down their guns when he turned to fire, but I fortunately did & thus prevented his shooting, as good fortune would have it. Had he shot, he would undoubtedly have killed one & perhaps more, for he would have fought to the last, but however many we might have killed, numbers would have overpowered us, & I would not be here to tell about it nor [would] Graham have become famous as leader of the riflemen in Cal. As soon as the indians [sic] came up to us, they took hold of us & shook us roughly, & finally made us sit down on the ground while they seated themselves in a circle about around us. They lit their pipe & passed it around, holding council for some time, no doubt about what should be done with us. By signs we tried to make them understand that we had slept with 80 indians whites, the night before not far from there [them?], hoping this might induce them to spare our lives. It had the desired effect, for they soon got up & made motions that we too should get up & lead them to our camp. We needed no second bidding but took the direction of camp with our captors. We soon reached our party, to our great relief & the Arapahoes camped near us. Our men had not suspected anything when they saw us coming in with the indians [sic], supposing that they were friendly to us. That night we doubled our guard & took every precaution to prevent a surprise. The night before we had done the same to guard against surpris[e] from our Pawnee friends & there was good cause for it, for they were prowling around our camp all night, & only the fact that we were on the alert & prepared, prevented them from attacking us.

The Arapahoes had gone into camp at about the same distance from us as the Pawnees the night previous. I was in [on] guard until twelve o'clock at night, & several times had seen indians [sic] prowling about that did not appear to me to be Arapahoe indians [sic], & upon being relieved by Price I told him, & cautioned him to keep a good lookout. It was not long before he saw an indian [Indian] approaching his post, whereupon he called out, "who[']s that." His answer was the snap of a gun lock & the flash of a flint. The indians [Indian's] gun did not go off, but Price Price's did, & we heard afterwards that an indian [Indian] was killed. At Price's shot, a yell arose on all sides of us & those who were on guard knew that the Pawnees were on us, as the Arapahoe camp had been perfectly quiet. Alex Sinclair, who was also on guard, called out, "Help, boys". We were all on our feet in an instant & our arms, which we always kept at our sides, ready for use. The indians [sic] fired a discharge of arrow[s] & a few shots among us, but did no further damage than to wound a few horses. We replied to their shots but as the night was dark we could not shoot with any certainty, for we could aim only when we saw the flash of their guns. We thought the Arapahoes also fired a few shots among us, but it was not positively known. We had a small cannon with us & having loaded it with 60 bullets we discharged it among it in the direction of our enemies, & succeeded in silencing their fire. We passed the rest of the night waiting for their return, but morning came & they had disappeared, & our friends the Arapahoes left us at an early hour. As a result of the night attack we found that we had lost 7 horses stolen, & as many more wounded. With the beginning of the firing our brave Col. Bean had been lost sight of, & also a man by the name of Williams. They became frightened & hid themselves. From that night time Col. Bean was totally disregarded & hardly treated civilly so that, soon after, when we got into New Mexico, he went to San Fernando & finally returned to Ark. with the first train of the regular traders that went back." Alex Sinclair was by [tacit?] agreement looked up to as our leader & continued in command until he was killed. He was a good leader although a trifle easy.
The arapahoes [Arapahoes] were all good looking indians [sic], somewhat larger than the Pawnees & armed & dressed about the same.

The country we had come through abounded in Buffalo & wild horses. The former roamed in immense herds. I have never seen so many cattle in in [sic] my life as I saw buffalo in that country. Wild horses were frequently captured by shooting them through the neck just above the spine, usually called creasing[.] This would knocked them down when the hunter would catch them before they could get onto regain their feet. The wound heals soon, & with a little training the horse soon became manageable.
Our meat was principally buffalo, although we occasionally killed deer & bear. In the Cross Timber, in the N. Fork was an there was abundance of turkeys & of wild honey.

The next morning after the Pawnees attacked us, we left our camp soon after the Arapahoes did theirs. Two days' travel brought us to the indian [Indian] village of our captors, where we remained one night[.] This village contained about 200 or 250 Warriors & perhaps as many women & children. We passed a sleepless night, & were rejoiced to leave our red friends behind the next morning at an early hour. From this point we had but 150 miles to go to reach the mountains. This we accomplished in about a week. Arrived at the foot of the Mts. we found plenty of feed & our party went into camp. Six of us were sent forward to examine the country before before [sic] us, & to search for a passable trail; as also feed for our animals.

Here we saw for the first time the black-tail deer. There were large numbers of them, so that we had no trouble in killing a considerable quantity for their meat, which we hung up to await the arrival of the main party. We slept one night in the Mts. & the following morning started back to camp. On the way, however, Dr[.] Craig, one of our party, gave out, so that Price & I were obliged to remain with him while the other three went to bring our party up. Before night they had returned with the rest of our company & we went into camp near where we had hung up the deer meat. Here too, the feed was quite plenty & it was decided to remain for a few days to allow the animals to recruit. From this place another advance party of six was sent out to cross the mountains & proceed as far a[s] the head waters of the Arkansas.

My brother Mark, a man by the name of Crispt, Graham, Basey, Dye, & I, were of the party. We [...] arrived at our journey's end without any adventure & camped in a beautiful valley in which the buffalo abounded, & feed was plenty. We immediately set to work to kill buffalo & soon had the satisfaction of seeing a large quantity, hung up out of the reach of the wolves & coyotes, ready for the coming of those behind.

As soon as this was done two of our party, Mark & Crispt, went back to camp to report, while we, who remained removed our camp[...] from the edge of the valley, where we had hung the buffalo meat, about two miles away & up the mountain's side. From this point we had a full view of the valley below, while our camp was hidden from those below, who might pass through the valley. From a small rise near our camp, we could at all times see our buffalo meat. This change of camp was made to guard against surprise from the indians [sic]. In an indian [Indian] country one cannot take too many precautions, & I owe my life many times over to my habitual vigilance & caution in all my movements while in the indian country mountains. Of the many trappers I have known that were killed in the mountains by indians [sic], a very large proportion of them were careless & imprudent in their habits. When out with a small force, I invariably ate supper & remained until dark in one place, & as soon as night came on moved away some distance to sleep.

We had been in our new camp but a few days when, having gone out on the rise to look towards our meat, I saw a war party of Arapahoes, of perhaps 80 or more, engaged in taking it down. This decided us to leave that dangerous neighborhood with all possible haste & make our way back to the remainder rest of our party. About half way back we met our companions at a former camp of the war party of the Arapahoes I had discovered seizing our buffalo meat. They had arrived there but a few hours before & the first objects that met their sight were the dead bodies of Mark, my brother, & Crist. They were scalped, & stabbed in several places. From these our party we learned that our two dead comrades had arrived safely at the camp informed theym where we were, & started back to find us. We were never able to find out the manner in which they were killed, but from some Mexicans to whom the Arapahoes sold their guns afterwards, we gleaned the following facts. The party that chased & captured Graham & me understood from the signs we made that there were 80 more white men back in the direction we pointed[...]. The fear of being punished by these 80 white men, should they kill us, no doubt induced them to spare our lives. When they reached our camp however, & found only 36 men, they at once concluded that the rest were coming up, & sent some of their best runners back to find them. These runners did not return until our party was already in the mountains. They reported having seen no more white men, whereupon a war party was immediately sent out to overtake & attack us. They fell in with my brother & Crisp [Crist], who, probably recognizing them as the same that had camped with in [us] before & through whose village we passed, & unsuspicious, travelled along with them, until an opportunity offered & the indians [sic] killed them. Of the precise circumstances immediately connected with their death, the Mexicans knew nothing, other than that my brother had killed 3 of the indians [sic] before they killed him.

Crispt was a quiet inoffensive man, but little versed in mountain life & with no skill in the use of arms. My brother, on the contrary, was one of the best men we had in our company[:] active, strong, hardy, brave, & a good shot. He was missed & mourned by all. This sad event led us to turn our course towards New Mexico, considering this section to[o] dangerous for our trapping operations.
During our journey from Fort Smith to the head of the Arkansas we found a few beaver, & at this time had collected about 40 or 50 skins.

As the country through which we must pass before reaching the settlements in New Mexico was almost destitute of grass for our animals, already somewhat reduced, we concluded to hide some of our traps, & having found a deep water hole, threw in about 60 or 70.

After entering New Mexico & just before we reached Arroyo Seco, a small Mexican town & our point of destination, we met the first signs of civilization, in a herd of tame cattle which we saw quietly grazing off to the right of our line of march, soon after we met a band of sheep driving driven by a young Mexican on horse back.

When we struck the head of the band they parted allowing us to ride in among them & without showing any fear at our presence. Not so with their herd herder, whose attention being diverted did not discover our presence until we were quite close to him, & when then with a startled glance he saw only that a party of horsemen were upon him, &, his fright preventing him from distinguishing us. He uttered a yell & put off for the neighboring mountains as fast as his horse could carry him. He took us for a war party of the Arapahoes, as these indians [sic] made frequent incursions into New Mexico in those times. The Arapahoes & Mexicans were deadly enemies, & although they would trade freely with each other, woe betide the poor Mexican who was caught by these savages, & the Mexicans spared no Arapahoe who might fall into their hands.

A few miles farther on we came to a shepperds [shepherd's] hut, its sole occupants an old man & a boy. The old man was the first to see us & he, like the young herded [herder], took us for Arapahoes[.]

He gave us one look & turning ran at the top of his speed to a ravine near by. He was some distance from the hut & had no time to call to the boy. By this time we were close to the hut, when they [the] boy, hearing us, came out, & recognizing us as white men advanced towards us & held out his had [hand], showing great pleasure at seeing us. The old man had by this time reached the opposite side of the ravine where he stopped to look back & seeing this boy among us, & realizing that we could not be indians [sic], he came back almost as fast as he had run away. He ran up to us, caught hold of our hands, & could hardly contain himself with joy. He proceeded to kill a sheep for us, & brought us out milk & corn cakes, which we ate with the greatest of relish.

Arroyo Seco was about 50 miles distant & we arrived there three days later. Here as at the shepperds [shepherd's] hut, we were received very hospitably, & in fact, every where in New Mexico, we always met with the same treatment. This seemed to be a great wheat country, & flour was in consequence quite cheap. Upon our arrival at Arroyo Seco they brought us a cart load of bread. There were quite a number of flour mills, also, in & around Arroyo Seco.

About 2 miles from Arroyo Seco was situated the town of San Fernando, quite a large place. The fur traders who came out annually from St[.] Louis had permanent trading posts established here. We went to Sn. Fernando very often but made our headquarters at Arroyo Seco. Having arrived here our party separated but 14 or 15 of the original company remaining together.

Those who left us here, as far as I can remember, were,— Col[.] Bean, who by this time was looked upon by all the company as the most insignificant among us. We had made a great mistake in choosing him for our leader, but the high estimation in which he was held by all, & his rank of Col. of Militia, led us to suppose him the best man. His brothers were well known to my family, my father having been with them in the early indian [Indian] wars. They owned the saltworks on the Ark. & were men of good standing.

William Bean also left us here with his father. He was a quiet, sensible young man, with none of his father's cowardice, & was very much liked by all. They both returned to Ark. with the first annual trading train that left San Fernando. Dr. Craig went into Sonora. About 8 or 10 joined Young's party at San Fernando & came to Cal. by the lower route, via Fort Yuma — Among these were Austin, a wild young fellow; Weaver; Hace,Wilkinson; the two Greens, Anderson, & Basey. The names of the others I do not remember. Anderson was killed by one of the Greens, not far from Los Angeles & just after they had entered Cal. While with us, Anderson, who was a large man, had imposed to a great extent upon Green, who was a small man, by continually constantly throwing his traps into the river & setting his own on Green's beaver signs. Green had warned him that, if he continued to treat him in that manner, that he would certainly kill him. To this threat, however, Anderson paid no heed, nor to the repeated warnings of others of the company, that Green would carry his threat into execution.
He continued to ill treat him until Green, put an end to him.

Near the place where he killed Anderson, he had set his traps for beaver, when Anderson, having come across them, threw them into the water, as he had repeatedly done before, & set his own traps in their place. Shortly after, Green visited his traps and, finding Anderson's, returned quietly to camp, walked up to him, & shot through the heart. Capt[.] Young gave Green up to the authorities at Los Angeles, but nothing was done with him, I believe, & he was allowed to leave the country.
Dr[.] Craig [...] is now living in San Francisco & came to see me about 7 or 8 years ago, stopping at my house for several days.

Among those of our party who were left, were the two Sinclairs; Graham; Price; Pollum, a Virginian; Nale; & myself; the others['] names I do not remember. At San Fernando, Rowland & Workman (of the La Puena Ranch, Los Angeles Co.) were living; they had already been there, I think, nearly 15 years. Rowland had a flour mill in that town. He was married to a Mexican woman & then had 3 or 4 children. I was well acquainted with the family, having often visited their house while I was in San Fernando. With Workman a [I] had less acquaintance[.] I was told that he, too, was married to a mMexican woman but I never visited his house. They afterwards removed to Cal. & settled in Los Angeles Co. Workman had a store in San Fernando. He sold clothing[,] provisions, &c. We did most of our trading with him. Besides these foreigners there were the two brothers Kincaid & an old man by the name of Chambers living in San Fernando.

Our party had arrived at Arroyo Seco early in March of '31. A few weeks later, Graham, Sinclair (Alex), & I decided to make an attempt to get the traps, [...] we had left on the Ark. Having secured the services of three Mexicans with their mules, & procured fresh animals for ourselves, we lost no time in starting on our trip. a [A] few days brought us within a short distance of our traps, but we found the snow still too deep on the mountains that lay between us & the river. At the same time an event occurred that caused us to retrace our steps with all possible haste. This was the presence in the vicinity of a large party of Arapahoes. We discovered them on a neighboring hill early one evening morning while we were in camp. Fortunately they did not see us, & as soon as [...] possible we packed up & made our way back to New Mexico, travelling day & night until we were well out of the indian [Indian] country. The Mexicans who accompanied us were very much frightened when they saw the indians [sic] & needed no urging to keep up with us in our retreat. We were somewhat disappointed in our failure to get the traps, as they were very dear at San Fernando.

Having sold the few skins we brought with us on our first arrival, we laid in a few supplies & in March ('31) our whole party now reduced to 14 or 15 men, set out for the Platte. On the North Fork we found a valley with beaver & here we remained until we trapped them out. On Sept. July 4 of '31, we returned to arrived at Arroyo Seco again with about two packs of skins of 60 each. These we sold in San Fernando, at $4 per pound, on an average of $10 per skin. In those days although there was a heavy duty on all beaver skins bro't [brought] into New Mexico, no one ever thought of paying them & as in our case, they would be smuggled into town in the night.

We fitted ourselves out a second time & in Sept. 1831 again started for the head waters of the Arkansas. Our party had been increased by the addition of 3 or 4 Mexicans, who had been hired by different members of our company. From Arroyo Seco to the Ark., we kept company with a band of trappers composed of French & Mexicans, about a doz. in all. On our way we saw a few Crow indians [sic], & had several horses stolen by them. We also saw numbers of the Snake Indians, who were then very friendly [to] the Pale faces.
Just before we reached the Ark. our camp was alarmed one night by the appearance in our midst of a young indian [Indian] woman of the Snake tribe.

An old Mexican who was sleeping near me was awakened in the night by some one passing near him, & reaching out his hand caught hold of her & held her fast, calling out at the same time — "I got one woman." We were all on our feet in an instant & with our arms in our hands ready to meet the expected attack, but no more indians [sic] appeared. We gave the poor woman plenty to eat & the next day she left us for her village not far away. We were able to learn that she had made her escape from the Kiowas, who had taken her prisoner, & had found the buffalo road which ran through our camp, & followed it until she was caught in our camp midst.
She had passed our guard without being seen.
We reached the head waters of the Ark. in Oct. without any further adventure. A few days more brought us to the Platte.

At this point the Mexican & French trappers left us, going South, down the Platte, while we crossed over to the Green river. Here [we] went into winter quarters in Nov. We had found a few beaver between the Ark. & Green rivers & had nearly a hundred when we went in to our quarters for the Winter. Buffalo we had found very scarce, but among the timber in the mountains an abundance of Elk. The place we had selected for our winter quarters was a longer[,] deep valley on the Green River about io miles wide & 20 in width length, & opening into a valley on the north & into another on the south. In this valley snow seldom fell & even then never remained. There was usually food for animals all the year around for when the grass grew scarce, or failed, the bark of the sweet cottonwod tree, of which there were immense quantities, [su]pplied its place. This valley was a favorite wintering place both of the whites&Indians. There was always buffalo in the valley so that we seldom [wan]ted for meat.

We remained here three months during which time we were confined to our valley by deep snows that everywhere surrounded it. We had nothing to occupy ourselves with, except the hunting of game for our supply of meat. About March of [18]32, we started out of the valley & followed the river towards its head, with the intention of trapping that season on the head waters of the Columbia. We trapped as we went along up the Green River, until May, when we learned that the place we intended going was already being trapped by other companies, whereupon we decided not to go there. The party now disagreed as to where we should go, & finally separated some going in one direction & some another, although the majority went together towards the Platte. At this place we met a trader by the name of O[.] Felon, an Irishman, who with a half doz. mules had brought liquor & a few articles such as blankets &c. to trade with the trappers. He was accompanied by [a] trapper by the name of Harris, & had 6 or 7 Mexicans to attend to his mules & packs. He was bound for Pierre's Hole, a deep valley situated between the Lewis & Henry's Forks, & the appointed rendezvous of the trappers & traders for that year. This would take place in July, so I determined to accompany O[.] Felon.

We arrived at Pierre's Hole just before the 4th of July. On [our] way we had passed crossed several mountains, & the last one just before reaching Pierre's Hole. Morning found us wading through the snow on its top & by evening we were in the midst of green grass & summer weather at its foot. At Pierre's Hole we found already arrived some 50 or more hunters & trappers. A few days later, a company of 150 (150) trappers under Wm Sublette arrived from St. Louis bringing supplies. The second in command, Fitzpatrick, they had lost while crossing Green River.
Having gone in advance to reconnoitre, he was cut off by the indians [sic] & so hard pressed that he was obliged to abandon his horse & take to the rocks. His companions supposed him killed.

A week or so after the arrival of the company [...] a trapper by name of Poe & I went out for a short hunt, & met Fitzpatrick crossing the Lewis Fork. He was mounted, having by the merest chance caught a horse saddled & bridled, that had escaped from one of the men at Pierre[']s Hole & wandered to where he was found by Fitzpatrick. Fitzpatrick was shoeless, hatless & almost naked. In crossing a river his powder horn was lost, & this rendered useless his gun & pistols, which he threw away. For ten days or thereabouts he had wandered about having in that time eaten of no food exxcepting a very small piece of dried meat. We piloted him back to camp. Other hunters, singly & by small companies, continued to arrive at the rendezvous until they numbered in all about 500. This was the favorite rendezvous for trappers West of the Rocky Mts. & had been the center of a rich beaver country. At this time however it was well nigh trapped out. The companies then at the rendezvous were as near as I can remember William Sublette[']s of 150 men they had come out expressly for trading purposes & returned with about 100 men to St[.] Louis when the rendezvous broke up; Milton Sublette is a brother of Wm, composed of about 30 men; Frapp's company, also about 30 men; Wyatt's company of emigrants of about 12 to 16; who were going to the mouth of the Columbia to explore country; Perkin's company of about 3 to 5 men. Our own company had also got together again, making some 14 or 15 more. When we separated on the Green River, the majority of them finally agreed to make a hunt on the Platte River, but having found the country filled with indians [sic] & lost one of their men by them, they turned back. The rest of the trappers at the rendezvous were a class that hunted singly in portions of the mts. Free from the indians [sic], or in unorganized bands & with no recognized leader many [of] these men never leaving the mountains. At the yearly rendezvous they would exchange their pelts for what few provisions supplies they required & then return to the mountains.

About the beginning of Aug. the trappers began to leave for their respective hunting grounds. Our party had decided to trap that season on the Marys River[,] a small stream about South West of Salt Lake. We left [...] Pierre[']s Hole in company with Frapp & Wyatt; our courses being the same for some distance. Frapp's company was mostly made up of canadian [Canadian] French halfbreeds. Our first camp was about 15 miles from the Rendezvous. Frapp's & Wyatt's companies camped together, while we were a short distance in their rear.

The next morning about 8 o'clock we packed up & rode along to Wyatt[']s & Frapp[']s camp, only a few hundred yards ahead, & had hardly rea reached it when indians [sic] were discovered coming towards us in large numbers, & we immediately recognized them as Blackfeet. They belonged to a village of some 400 warriors or more, that with their women[,] children, & camp baggage were moving north. They had discovered us before we did them no doubt & had resolved on attacking us. They were ridding down on us at full speed & barely gave us time to prepare for them. We hurriedly formed a breastwork of our packs & despatched a young boy on our fleetest horse back to Pierre's Hole for success aid. We saw from their numbers that we would need help & but [?] by holding them indians [sic] in check for two or three hours we knew reinforcements would reach us. As soon as the indians [sic] arrived within range they began shooting, to which we replied. Conspicuous among them was a chief dressed in a bright scarlet coat & he rode somewhat in advance of his men, who began to scatter & surround us upon arriving within shooting distance. On came the chief & out rode one of Wyatt[']s men, Goddor, a canadian [Canadian] half-breed, to meet him. Across his saddle Goddar carried a short rifle which the chief did not see until, when within 40 or 50 yds. of him, Goddar raised it & shot. The chief fell from his saddle dead & before his companions could come up to him [...] his coat was stripped off by Goddar who amidst a heavy fire reached our camp in safety with his trophy. We continued to exchange shots, with a loss to the indians [sic] of one or two killed, & to us of several wounded, until about ten o'clock when they [the] indians[sic] suddenly took shelter in the heavy narrow belt of woods that lay between us & the river. We soon discovered the cause of this unexpected movement, in the coming of our reinforcements that began to appear in sight & a few minutes latter [later] were among us to the number of about 250. Most of them were without saddles having lost no time in setting out as soon as our messenger reached them. A council was held & Wm Sublette was elected as our leader. Many were opposed to attacking them as being posted in the heavy timber we would find it difficult to drive them out & our loss would be considerable. These objections were overruled by Sublette & others, who said we would have to fight them anyway & now that we had them at a disadvantage we must profit by it.

The plan of attack was formed & the attacking party got into line & advanced when the firing at once became general &[.] Just after we entered the timber our captain Alex. Sinclair was shot in the thigh," Phelps a man who joined us at Pierre[']s Hole, was wounded in about the same place, & Wm Sublette was shot in the arm. Our attacking party did not consist of much over 100 men [,] the rest refusing to join us. As we advanced & drove the indian [sic] towards the river, the wings of our line gradually [...] turned in until they rested on its bank & we had them surrounded.

Upon penetrating into timber we found that the Blackfeet had constructed a fort of logs on the bank of the river in the form of a halfmoon the rear opening towards the river. We continued to advance[,] dodging & crawling from tree to tree & log to log[...] every foot stubbornly contested by the redskins, until almost sunset. Some of our men had succeeded in getting in the rear of the fort, whichstill however afforded its inmates some shelter even onthe [on the] open side, as it was filled with trees. One of the trappers of Frapp[']s company got very near the rear of the fort, almost up to it in fact, by crawling flat on the ground & pushing & rolling a large log so as to protect his head. Several shots struck the log but the trapper got into the position & abandoned his log for a tree without being harmed.

Another one of Frapp's men, a Canadian halfbreed, tried to distinguish himself by rashly crawling up to the very wall of the fort & then peeping over the top. He paid for his temerity with his life. He had barely raised his head above the breastworks of logs when he received two bullets in his forehead. He was half drunk at the time, liquor having been distributed among the men during the early part of [the] fight. By sunset we had got so close to the fort that we determined to set it on fire but before doing so it was agreed to give the indians [sic] a chance to surrender. Accordingly, a renegade Blackfoot who was among Frapp's men was instructed to talk with them & try & induce them to surrender. They refused, however, & answered that, although they would all be killed that day, the next day it would be our turn, as they had sent word to a very large village of their nation situated only a short distance from there numbering some 1500 lodges. It was well known that there was a very large village, &nearby & that should they send out all of their force after us there would be some heavy fighting in which we would in all probability get worsted. Upon hearing the answer of the indians [sic] Frapp became alarmed & withdrew his men at once & this obliged the rest of us to retire & those from Pierre's Hole having returned, we travelled on about 9 miles & went into camp with our camp the same companies as the night previous. The next morning several of us went back to the scene of the fight[.] Within the fort & its immediate vicinity the ground was strewn with dead bodies mostly of women & children; but a very few warriors among them.

They We counted 50 dead bodies, & inside of the fort were the bodies of 20 fine horses. Of the from 300 to 400 indians [sic] which it was calculated the fighting men of the Blackfeet numbered but very few escaped.

At the beginning of the engagement several got away, many of them being shot in attempting to swim the river. We afterwards learned through the indians [sic] that when we withdrew our [?] only 6 indians [sic] were left alive in the fort. The dead [...] indians [sic] were thrown into the river to prevent them from falling into our hands.
Many of the women were shot unintentionally as in the timber it was impossible to distinguish the women from the men; the children were killed no doubt by stray shots.
The indians [sic] make a very poor fight on foot, their usual mode of fighting being to lay in ambush or cut off small detached partyies, with such numbers as to make success sure.

We lost no time in getting out of this neighborhood, pushing forward as rapidly as possible. A few days latter [later] we struck buffalo & halted to get meat. We also furnished meat to Wyatt, whose men were mostly green & in these matters[.]

Having got a sufficient supply we continued our journey without further adventure. We parted with Wyatt's company about 100 mls [miles] from Pierre's Hole & with Frapp just north of Salt Lake. We trapped on the Marys River with fair success until Oct., when we went North & intending to winter in the Green River Valley where we had passed the previous winter. We found it already occupied by some of the Snake indians [sic], & buffalo very scarce so that we determined upon making no permanent quarters but move about from place to place. We visited the head waters of the Red & Yellowstone rivers & trapped a few beavers between the Green & Platte rivers. The cold was very severe. When up near the head waters of the Yellowstone we had several horses stolen by the indians [sic] & knowing with almost a certainty that the Crows were the thieves we visited their villages in the hopes of recovering our property; but unsuccessfully offered them a reward to return them.

A white man by the name of Ballard was living among the Crow indians [sic] & served as interpreter[.] It was rumored that he was a fugitive from justice. During the same winter we met a party of 40 Crow indians [sic]. Their number emboldened them, so that they acted [very?] saucily. One of them a large powerful fellow took a fancy to my powder horn. He made signs for me to give it to him & upon being refused he took out his knife & was about to cut the string with which it was hung from my shoulder & take it. I had my hand on the handle of my knife & was determined to kill him the moment he cut the string, at the same time I called out to our men to look out for themselves. Our Capt. spoke some crow [Crow] tongue & warned the indian [Indian], who immediately desisted from his design, all of our men having at that time laid hold of their rifles. The indians [sic] saw that we meant to fight them if necessary & they wisely let us alone[.]
This nation is one of the most thieving nation[s] of all those that live in the mountains. We lost several horses by them & were never able to find any of them.

In April of the following year we went into the waters of the North Fork of the Platte. Here we encamped & had our horses picketed a few hundred yards away. One morning, having gone out with a companion to bring in horses we discovered a band of something like 80 Rees[e] indians [sic] ridding [riding] down on us. We had barely time to get back to camp before the indians [sic] reached the stock, which they immediately drove off. Here a man by [the] name of P Gillum was, it was supposed, killed by these indians [sic]. He had a very fine horse picketed out in another direction & contrary to all advice, persisted in going for it. We never found him again. This mishap obliged us to travel over 300 miles on foot back to the Green river valley[,] the place appointed for the rendezvous the next year. We did not winter in the valley however but wandered about from place to place as we had done the previous winter. The cold this season was very severe, at times even at midday it was very uncomfortable[.]

This winter's experience decided me, [as?] also some others of our company, to seek a warmer climate, & having heard many wonderful stories of California we settled upon coming here. In the Spring, there were a large number of trappers gathered [...] at the rendezvous in Green River Valley & among them Capt. Walker & Company, bound for California. We joined him, making a party in all of 36. Upon the breaking up of the rendezvous we started Southward intending to trap a short time on the Marys River. The indians [sic] troubled us so much however that we found it impossible to remain[;] they stole our traps & made it necessary to be continually on our guard to prevent an attack. They became very bold & at last offered to let us go through their country unmolested if we would give them our horses & meat. They spoke the Snake tongue, a language which most of our men were familiar with. We continued to travell [ravel] along & they followed us, garthering [gathering] additional force, as the[y] proceeded from other villages. Just before we gave up trapping entirely they shot at one of our men by [the] name of Frazier, while he was setting his traps, & it was only by the veriest chance that he escaped them. From this time on we doubled our guards & our precautions, making detours from the trail when necessary to avoid passing through narrow defiles, thickly wooded places & all other places favorable for an ambuscade. A few days before reaching the Sierra Nevada mountains we found that our trail passed through a large, thick, body of willo[w]s, and, as we had seen the indians [sic] around the day before, we determined to avoid the Willows by making a detour in the adjoining plain. This precaution saved some lives if not those of the whole party. Hardly had the trail been left when the indians [sic] to the number of 400 to 500, emerged from the thicket, they formed in several distinct bodies or companies representing no doubt the respective villages to which they belonged.

We halted & prepared for a fight. Thirty four of the indians [sic] advanced in a body, & 15 of our men, myself among the num, were ordered out to meet them. From 50 to 60 yds[.] from our company we halted & awaited the indians [sic]. We allowed them to get quite close before opening fire, but when we did shoot it was with such telling effect that but one of the 34 escaped. This seemed appeared to completely disenhearten our enemies for they permitted us to pass without further opposition. After entering the mountains, I went ahead one day with the Capt[.], & another of our men to select a camping place with water; we became somewhat separated in our search, & upon entering the timber I discovered fresh signs of indians [sic]. This alarmed me somewhat as I feared for the Capt. & our companion who like myself had probably each taken a different course. I had just begun to look about for more signs when glancing back in the direction I had come I saw two indians [sic], with heads down & at a trot coming along my trail[.] I supposed that they were following my tracks, so I lost no time in getting behind a tree & preparing for them. It took them some few minutes to reach me & in the meantime they would stop every few yards & look back, & listen as if pursued. I saw that they did had not seen me or discovered my tracks as they passed within a few feet of me jabbering as they went along. I at first had a notion to let them go but the murdering death of my dead brother so treacherously murdered by these red devils, was too fresh in my mind. The indians [sic] were travelling in single file, & watching my chance, just before they would have to turn around a small point of rocks, I fired shooting both of them dead at the first shot. I took their blankets, the only articles they had worth taking, as they were armed with bows, & returned to the company. This was the last of the indians [sic]. In June of 1834 we cross[ed] the Sierra Nevada mountains & came down through a valley between the Merced & Tuolom[n]e rivers, into the San Joaquin Valley. Here we found an abundance of elk, deer, & bear. In Nov. following, we arrived at Gilroy's Ranch & went into camp close by, where we remained about a month.

While here the men employed themselves in hunting deer for the pelts. Leaving our camp at Gilroy's, we proceeded to Monterrey, where we arrived about the middle of Dec. Here we spent Christmas. Shortly after, Capt. Walker returned to the mountains with some 20 men. He was one of the best leaders I have ever met, a good hunter & trapper, thoroughly versed in indian [Indian] signs & possessed of good knowledge of the mountains. He could find water quicker than any man I ever met.

Later he returned to Cal. on a secon[d] trip. I remained in Monterey with a few others of our company. Here I met Yount, who had been in Cal[.] some time[.] he [He] was about to return to S. F. & invited me to go with him & to make an otter & beaver hunt around the Bay & up the San Joaquin River. I sent my baggage in a Russian brig & I accompanied Yount in his canoe. We hunted a little on the Petaluma side of [the] Bay & then proceeded to the San Joaquin river, where we trapped with very fair success for about two mos. We returned with about 30 beaver, 2 sea otter & 14 land otter. Sea otter skins where [were] then worth $30 (ea), land otter $2, & beaver about $4. While on this hunt, we found among the Tulares a little indian [Indian] girl that had been abandoned by it[s] parents probably.

One afternoon having found a strip of dry land among the tules, we decided to camp there for the night. As we landed Yount saw what appeared to be indian [Indian] huts about a quarter of a mile above us on the same strip of land, & while he got supper I set out to reconnoitre. Arriving at the huts I looked into them all, but found them empty. I passed on some distance but saw no signs of indians [sic][.] It was dusk when I started on my return. In repassing the huts I heard a moan then others in succession which I traced to one of the huts. Looking in I saw nothing at first, but my eyes soon becoming accustomed to the darkness I made out a small child seated in the farther corner of the hut. I went in & the little thing tried to talk to me but I could of course understand nothing of its language. I left it & returned to camp, where I found Yount somewhat alarmed at my absence & who, when I told him of the child & proposed to go back & get it, would not listen to me. He was afraid the Indians might return in the night. All night it seemed as though I could hear the little one's cries. Early in the morning I went to the huts & found the the [sic] little one so weak that she could not sit up. Upon bringing her out of the hut we found her nothing but skin & bone. She had probably been without food for three or four days. We took her to camp & gave her a piece of boiled beaver, & it was pitiful to see the eagerness with which she caught it to her mouth & sucked at it voraciously. We feared to allow her to eat too much at first & so took the meat from her after she had sucked it a few minutes, but so tightly did she have it pressed to her mouth that main force was necessary to take it from her. We made clothes for her & with a little care she soon recovered. When we returned from our hunt Yount took her home with him, after having her baptized & christened at the mission of San Francisco.

A few weeks after our return to S. F. Yount took a contract from Capt. A[.] B[.] Thompson of Santa Barbara to furnish him with 20,000 shingles for his hotel which was then being built & is now known as the San Carlos. He offered to hire me but I informed him that I was not in the habit of working for wages. I soon after took passage on the "California", Alfred Robinson, supercargo, & came here to Santa Barbara. Here I met a hunter by the name of Sills, an old acquaintance of the mountains & who had come to Cal. with Gant. We agreed to hunt together on the Islands. At that time it was impossible for newcomers to procure a license. Capt. Denny the Capt [.] of this port, had a license, and Burton, Sparks & other hunters then here hunted under his license, paying him a share of the skins.

We had made the same arrangement with Capt[.] Denny & 8 or 10 days after I arrived here Sills & I went to Santa Rosa Island. We had no boats so were obliged to hunt from land. We went over about May of 1835. Two weeks later Sills was taken sick & returned to Santa Barbara. I remained about six weeks longer & killed in all 8 or 10 otter, Sills having got none. I had with me a Kanaka indian [Indian], employed to swim out for the otter killed; [...] at $16 a month.
I hunted under Capt. Denny's license for about a year & a half or two years. Burton & Sparks had just come from a hunt on the Islands, when Sills & I went over to Sta. Rosa.

Soon after his return Sparks went up to Monterey to see a new schooner that was being built by a Mexican at that place. The schooner's name was to be "Peor es Nada," of about 20 tons burthen. He charted her for a trip on the Lower California coast; a man by name of Charley Hubbard being put in command by the owners.

Sparks was accompanied by Burton & a hunter by name of Dye. They returned about Sept[.]; I think, having made a very poor hunt owing to their failure to agree among themselves. They got I believe only 25 skins when they should have secured 200 at least as the otter were plenty, the N. W. indians [sic] [not] having been there for some years. The N. W. Indians did hunt along that coast afterward & killed a great many otter. In the following Oct. after the return from the Leeward, as Lower Cal. was called, Sparks went over to San Nicolas Island. Others accompanied Sparks & among them Williams, of the Chino ranch in Los Angeles Co. & who was with me in the Mts. he, with Col. Bean, having shown the white-feather in our first engagement with the indians [sic], on the Ark. river. They removed the ind.s [sic] some 17 or 18 men[,] women & children, from this Island to San Pedro, & thence to Los Angeles & San Gabriel. I have heard from Sparks an account of the affair but do not remember the details distinctly. Some one in Los Angeles authorized the removal of these indians [sic], the last of the inhabitants of the San Nicolas, but with what object I do not know, & cannot remember if I h ever heard. I am sure Williams had an interest in the matter as he afterwards took one of the indian [Indian] women to live with. Having got the ind.s [sic] together on the Island they took them to the beach & put them on board the schooner. They then took them direct to San Pedro having however left one indian [Indian] woman on the Island. Of the exact manner in which she was left I do not now remember; but am under the impression that Sparks told me that it happened in this way. Having got all of the indians [sic] down on the beach, one of the women wanted to go back to their rancheria for her child that had been left behind, which she was allowed to do. While she was absent a strong wind sprang up & fearing for the safety of the schooner should they wait longer they put off from shore & ran before the wind. Arriving safely at San Pedro, the indians [sic] were landed, from whence they proceeded to Los Angeles, where a portion of them remained; the rest being taken to San Gabriel. One of the indians [sic], however, a large powerful man, was left at San Pedro. He lived on the beach among the hunters, where I saw him several times. I think he was one of the most muscular men, white or indian [Indian], I ever saw. He was but a little above the medium height, heavy set & full & broad shoulders & chest. He was partly foolish from a fracture of the skull received in a fight with the N. W. Indians, but he was perfectly harmless & invariably good humored. He was always willing to work cheerfully performing the most fatiguing tasks ofttimes without being solicited. If a boat was to be hauled ashore he would frequently rush into the water, catch hold of the boat & run it high & dry on the beach &a feat that is usually required from 3 to 4 ordinary men to perform. I also saw him take under his arm & bring a considerable distance to the shore a spotted seal that had been shot from shore. This seal would weigh not less than 300 to 400 lbs., besides being very awkward to handle. It would have required 3 men at least to bring it ashore. I never heard what became of him. The schooner "Peor es Nada", was ordered to report at Monterey immediately to take a cargo of timber to S. F. & thus no time was given them to return to the island for this woman, although I think it was the intention of this same party to go back as soon as possible.

The "Peor es Nada sailed for San Francisco with timber & was capsized or foundered off the Golde[n] Gate, her crew, being saved. It was afterwards reported that having drifted to sea she was picked up by the Russians, but nothing definite was ever known.

Throughout the entire length of the coast it was known that an indian [Indian] woman had been left on the Island of San Nicolas but no attempt was ever made to rescue her or to learn her fate & [as] years passed on, all agreed that she must have perished.

For many years after the loss of the "Peor es Nada, the only craft on the coast where [were] small boats to which the long distance & rough sea of the outer rim of the channel would render a trip extremely dangerous.
Eighteen years elapsed before anything farther was done to ascertain her fate; when in 1853 she was found alive & brought to Santa Barbara.

After my return from the Islands, I met a negro [sic][,] an otter hunter who had been here some time having deserted from the Pilgrim a trading vessel from Boston, his proper name was Allen Light (he was very dark skinned) but here was always called "Black Steward." He was quite intelligent, well behaved & mannerly, & a good hunter. With him I made a hunt up the coast as far as Point Concepcion. We each had a boat & one Kanaka. Made a very short trip & got 21 otter skins.

When Sparks return[ed] from the Lower Coast he & the Black Steward & I agreed to hunt together & were taken over to Santa Rosa by the "Peor es Nada" immediately after the indians [sic] were taken off San Nicolas. We remained all winter on the Islands making our headquarters at Santa Rosa, although hunting on San Miguel & Santa Cruz as there were very few otters on Santa Rosa. We got altogether on this hunt 60 skins. I also made a second trip up the coast with Black Steward before going on the Islands with Sparks. We went as far up as San Luis Obispo; were gone 3 or 4 months & got 50 otter skins.
We paid Capt[.] Denny 40% of our skins, he furnishing our provisions & paying the wages of one man for each hunter.

About [the] first of January 1836 we had a fight with N. W. Ind.s [sic] on the head of the Santa Rosa Island. There were on the Island at the time Sparks, Black Steward, & I, hunters of our party; O Brien an Irishman, Mathers an American[,] 3 Kanakas, & Harry Plomer an Englishman, & our Cook. Besides our party there was a Portuguese called Manuel also a hunter & 2 Kanakas as help, making in all 12 men[.]

On the N. E. side of the Island & close to the present wharf there is a large cave. Its entrance is hardly larger than an ordinary doorway, but so large inside that a hundred persons could occupy it with ease. Here we kept our provisions & other supplies. About [the] first of January (1836) Sparks & some of our men saw a brig, one day, in the upper part of the Channel, & remarked casusually that they were perhaps N. W. Inds. Coasting or trading vessels being frequently seen in the Channel & the N. W. Inds. not having visited these parts for some time, we all took it for granted that the craft seen was a trading vessel. This appearance of the N. W. Ind.s would not have surprised us, as we knew they were likely to come at any time, & having talked the matter over long before, we had agreed to fight them at least as long as we could; to this the Portuguese also agreed. Sparks & Black Steward, while hunting together before, had been driven up into the Island by these indians [sic] & their supplies captured, but we determined to defend ours as long as it could be done.

One morning a few days after sighting the brig we were hunting off the head of the Santa Rosa. It was very foggy & at about 7 o'clock we started an otter & began running it towards the head of the Island. Black Steward was about 1/4 mile from shore, I was nearly opposite him & distant about 300 or 400 yds. farther out while Spark[s] was between us & a little to the rear. Just as we were rounding the point the Black Steward called out[, "]Here come the N. W. Inds.["] Sure enough just ahead of us, coming out of the fog were 5 or 6 canoes pulling with might & main to cut us off from the shore. Each canoe had two indians [sic] & some of them a third. When Black Steward called to us the foremost canoe was but a few hundred yards away & the other only a short distance in the rear.

The fog had prevented us from discovering them, while our shooting had indicated to them our exact position. At the first alarm we made a straight line for the shore & our men needed no urging to exert themselves. We all made for a small cove or bay just below the point & lined with thick bushes. Black Steward was the first to reach the beach. Jumping out as soon as his boat grounded, he turned & fired on the foremost canoe, but the powder having partly escaped from his gun the ball fell short. A moment later Sparks reached shore & almost at the same time I jumped out on the beach beside him amidst a shower of buckshot the inds [sic] having already opened fire. At that moment they were first canoe was not over a hundred yards away & the others close behind. Sparks fired at the foremost canoe wounding on[e] of the inds. [sic], who fell, but raised again just fin time to receive my shot which settled him. This was a reception they little expected & they turned back until a safe distance from us, exchanging shots with us in the meanwhile. As soon as each of us fired his first shot we took refuge in the bushes under cover of which we soon drove them out of range. We killed 3 & wounded 4 or 5 of them while none of us received a scratch. All of our men excepting O Brien had made for the hills as soon as we landed, not even waiting to haul[...] the boats up, & their steps hastened by the buckshot that had already begun to whizz around us. O Brien was given a gun but he was not a very good shot. The whole number of canoes in the attacking party was 13, each canoe having two men & many of them an extra man. In a canoe there were generally two guns but not unfrequently they carried three. In hunting otter the[y] generally used buck shot, their arms being the old English musket, & this is the reason why the[y] first used buckshot with us. After the first shot they loaded with ball as they saw we were determined to make a stand. The range of these guns is something incredible. Our men assured us that the bullet from the indians [Indians'] guns passed them when they were fully a mile from the beach. The canoes having gotten out of range, they rested for a few minutes & then pulled off to the Brig which we could now plainly see about a mile away, the fog having lifted. We then left our cover, drew the boats well up on the beach & buried them in the sand, likewise our provisions, lest the indians [sic] should return. This being down [done] we moved back to a hollow a short distance from the shore where we could watch the vessel without being seen, & awaited further developments. The rest of the day passed without any further movements on the part of the indians [sic].

At nightfall most of our men returned to our camp. We kept watch that night but saw or heard nothing. Daybreak discovered to us the Brig about 2 or 3 miles away[.] About 9 a.m. we saw them lower their canoes eleven in all & start towards shore. Arriving within a short distance they began to paddle about as if in search of otter. They continued to manoeuvre about in this manner for some time. They gradually approached the cave[,] passed by it & repassed it as if without any intention of landing[;] finally they proceeded to a point 300 or 400 yds. below & then stopped to fish in the kelp just opposite it. Loth to lose this chance, we instructed Black Steward & O Brien to remain & keep a lookout while we crept down to the point to get a shot at them if possible. We reached the point unseen & were about to fire when the men at the cave raised the cry that the inds [sic] were landing. We ran back just in time. Just before we reached the cave Black Steward & O'Brien both fired at the two indian [sic] in the first canoe but missed them. Our shots brought down one of them, whereupon [they] turned & put off firing as they went. They again went off to the Brig. The two days following the brig lay becalmed without any further attempt of the inds [sic] to return. On the third they sailed away & we never saw them again. On the morning of the second day as soon as the canoes started for shore our men made for the hills again but returned that same evening, & after the Brig was out of sight we went back to the cave.

During all this time we had not been there lest the inds. [sic] should see us & perhaps suspect that we had our supplies there. Three days after our fight the valiant Portuguese, Manuel, came to camp half starved having wandered about the interior of the Island without any food. At the sound of our first shots he & his Kanakas took to the hills & did not venture back until they saw the Brig leave. The first day they were sure we had all been killed & the second day they were surprised to think that there was any of us left to fight. They could hear the firing distinctly although from 2 to 3 miles away.

This defeat was a severe blow to the N. W. Inds. who for several years had been the terror of the Coast. This was the first reverse they had met with. They had been in the habit of running [off] hunters on the Islands & stealing their supplies & furs. On the main land they landed when & wherever the[y] liked, killing cattle & horses & occasionally the inhabitants; so I have been told. They usually came in brigs or larger vessells [vessels] well armed with cannon; these same vessels being almost invariably owned or fitted out by Americans or English & manned & officered by men of the same nationalities, the Indians being employed to hunt[.] Landing occasionally on the Islands they attacked the almost defenseless natives killing many of them as the piles of human bones on these Islands[,] especially on that of San Nicolas, abundantly testify. Sometime before I arrived in this country, I am told the Mexican Govt. fitted out a vessel for the purpose of guarding the Coast, preventing contraband & especially to capture some of these N. W. otter hunters. She was lying at Santa Barbara when the Bolivar, an American vessel engaged in otter hunting, dared an him to attack running close by the Govt[.] vessel, her six guns shotted, & with matches burning. The Capt. of [the] Govt. vessel acknowledged that he could do nothing, having no good fighting men among his crew. The year before I came here, Sparks told me that, an American brig came down with N. W. Indians & anchored near Santa Rosa where he & several other hunters were at the time. Some one proposed to capture her, to which all gave a willing assent. There were Sparks, Higgins, Thompson[,] Joaquin a Portuguese, & about 8 others who joined in the plan. Thompson was to be the leader. They started of[f] in the night[,] reached the brig, & where [were] about to board her when hearing a noise, Thompson was afraid that they were discovered & insisted upon giving it up much to the disgust of Sparks & two or three others who did not believe in that way of doing things, but the majority were of the fame [same] opinion as the Capt[.] or leader & so they returned to shore.

A year after the fight with the N. W. Inds. we ran across two of this crew of the brig from whom we learned that she was an English ship commanded by Capt. Bancroft & that her crew was mostly foreigners.

They told us that in the first day's fight we had killed 3 & wounded 4 or 5 of their inds [sic] & on the second day had wounded one, who received two bullets in the thigh within a 1/4 of an inch of each other, which were no doubt made by Sparks & I, as we fired both at the same time & both of us good shots.

They had not used their cannon, of which they had several, because they could not seen us. On the second trip of this brig, something over a year after we learned that, owing to some trouble about rations, the indians [sic] rose & killed the Capt. & also shot his wife, who interposed her body between her husband & his murderers. She was half Kanaka; & her death was supposed to be accidental[
