Long before I came here, the N. W. Inds. had hunted the otter along the whole length of the coast from Lower Cal. north & they must have killed a very large number, as in early times they were so abundant that the indians [sic] killed them with spears.

When I arrived here they were still quite plenty but they were soon hunted out. In hunting, the N. W. Inds[.] used buckshot & usually sent several canoes together so that they could almost surround an otter, thereby rendering escape almost impossible.

About the 10th or 12th of April 1836 we started from Santa Cruz to Santa Barbara [...]an unusually when a very strong South Easter sprang up & it was with difficulty that we made land at G[...] Goleta several miles above Sta. Barbara. During the same storm, Capt. Hinckley's bark, a merchant vessel, went ashore at the mouth of the Arroyo Burro 1 1/2 miles N. of the Light House.

A few weeks later we again fitted out this time for a trip up the coast. When we reached Piedra Blanca, Sparks returned to Santa Barbara, bought Foxen[']s house near the site of Cranes Hall & opened a tradding store. Black Steward & I continued up the coast, & did not return until Nov. following. We got about 60 skins. This was the last trip we made under Capt. Denny's license.

Two weeks after our return, Alvarado & his force arrived here on their way to Los Angeles. I was then staying at Burton & Branch[']s, at the time the only boarding place in town, there being no hotel. Alvarado & Graham came to see us. Alvarado offered us $2 per day & the privilege of taking up vacant lands if we would join him. I accepted the offer. Alvarado's force consisted of about 100 men; 40 were foreigners under Graham, whose Lieut. was John Cappinger an Englishman who had served [in] the English army; the remain[ing] 60 were Californians. Among the foreigners whose name[s] I can [now] remember were, Thompson who [had] hunted with Sparks, two Portuguese, Manuel & Joaquin. We remained here a few days, perhaps 8 or 10, & then proceeded to San Buenaventura. Thence, after being delayed two or three days by a storm, we marche[d] towards San Fernando. On the way we received several threatening messages from Rocha; who commanded the Don Carlos Carrillo party at San Fernando, assuring us of this his determination to destroy us if we persisted in advancing. Not in the least intimidated, we continued our march to San Fernando until within 6 miles of the Mission, where we camped for the night. We prepared for a fight the next morning, not doubting that after so many threats, a show of resistance, at least, would be made.

The next morning we continued our march until we arrived on the plain within half a mile of the Mission. Here we halted, the foreigners dismounting & forming into line preparatory to advancing to the attack of the enemy, who to the number of about a hundred, were already formed outside the Mission, in full view of us. As soon as our line was formed, the order was given to advance. The enemy broke & ran before we were half way to them. They were all well mounted & they did not spare their horses. The last we saw of them, fully two miles away, they were still running at full speed. We made no attempt to follow them. We took possession of the old mission, & its only inmate an old man; Don Igno. del Valle's father[.]

After 3 nights['] stay at San Fernando we proceeded to Los Angeles. Here we met several of Don Carlos Carrillo's party & among them Roach, who assured us that it had was his intention to fight us at San Fernando but he could not make the men stand. I am inclined to think that they did not care to face the foreigners for whom they had a wholesome respect, especially the riflemen or hunters. A week later we left Los Angeles for Santa Barbara. Upon our arrival a[t] this latter place we each, Black Steward & I received [from] $30 to $40, from Alvarado.

Soon after, we resumed our hunting, making a trip up the coast as far as Monterrey. On this trip we were accompanied by a man by the name of Simmonds, who had learned to hunt & shoot a little having been with us on wages as in one of the boats for several trips. We were about 2 1/2 mos. in reaching Monterrey & got something like 50 skins. We found a ready sale for them to a German trader by name of Myers. He paid Black Steward & Simmonds cash for their skins, but asked me to wait for my pay until I should return to Santa Barbara, which I did, & where he afterwards paid me. Simmonds no sooner received his money than he took to drinking & left us. Myers paid us $35 apiece/ea[ch] for our skins, & I believe, sent them to Mexico. Black Steward & I hunted back killing about 30 otter. Several months after Simmonds returned to Santa Barbara[.] completely broke, having not only spent all his money but also sold his gun & boat.

This year (1837) I decided to avail myself of Alvarado's offer by taking up a ranch; as for a long time I had determined to settle in the country. I had wandered of over the greater portion of this part of Cal. & had seen no section that suited me better than what is now San Luis Obispo County. Having arrived at Branch's Ranch, whose owner had invited me to make [it] my headquarters, I started out in search of a location & immediately found the Wasner ranch, which suited me very well. I hunted about a little longer not knowing but that I might find a place that would suit me still better. I was nearly a year hunting, & searching for a ranch during which time I spent over $500. Soon after selecting the site of the Wasner ranch, I went to see Alvarado but was put off with some excuses & so a second[,] third & a fourth time until being pressed to give me a definite answer, he [said] that notwithstanding his promise it would be impossible for him to give me a grant of land, owing to a law recently passed by the General Govt. that only those foreigners married to Californians could take up lands. I had, in the meantime, employed Capt. Denny to make a plan of the locality, which I brought with me to Santa Barbara. Sometime afterwards Sparks induced me to give him the plan promising to get the grant for me & since the site was plenty large enough, we could divide it between us. He in some manner obtained the grant but never gave me one acre of it. At this time game was very plenty in San Luis Obispo Co. especially deer & grizzly bear. Of the latter I killed 45 this year (1837). The natives hunted them occasionally but for their mode of hunting, with the lasso, required open ground, while the grizzly seldom left the thickets & timber. Here I had several adventures with bear. My horse which I usually rode was a large[,] powerful one. One day while riding through a dense thicket not far from Branch's ranch, a she bear with two yearlings sprang upon us, so near that my horse barely had time [to] jump one side, turn & dash out of the thicket. All this the horse did of his own accord, as I did not see the bears at that moment. I attempted to control him but found it impossible, as the bears wewer [were] so close behind that I think for a moment the old one had hold of his tail[.] Just before the horse reached the clearing he ran under an oak tree whose lower limbs knocked me off, one of them hitting me in the forehead & almost knocking me insensible. I laid perfectly quiet where I fell & all three of the bears rushed past me so close that I could have touched them. I lost no time in getting up a tree knowing that they would soon return. Having followed the horse to the edge of the thicket, they turned back sniffing & grunting until they reached my tracks, which they soon followed to the tree. Look- [Looking] up the tree, they discovered me & raised up on their hind legs, growling fiercely & scratching the bark off.

They soon left me & went back into [the] middle of the thicket. I returned to the ranch[,] procured another horse & some dogs & returned. I found my horse about a half a mile from this thicket. A Mexican had come with me so after finding my horse we returned to the thicket. We made the dogs go in & followed with our horses a short distance. The bears chased us out into the open ground. We repeated this until I got a good chance & killed one of the young ones. I did not dare attack the old one as my horse was almost unmanageable through fright, he having been chased & nearly caught once before by a grizzly, & beside of my I was still suffering from several bad bruises & cuts in my head & face. I could have shot one of them while I was in the tree, but was afraid that the others would climb after me. About two mos[.] after this affair, I discovered the old one & remaining cub one day eating acorns when creeping upon them I shot the old one first & then the cub. I knew the old one by its having one short ear. Another day having seen fresh tracks, I followed them down into a hollow with a thick strip of willows running through it. I had almost reached them when I saw four full grown bears. I quietly turned about & rode back. They saw me & set up a loud grunting but did not attempt to follow me neither did I care to go after them. Mr[.] Branch had been chased several times by grizzls [grizzlies] narrowly escaping them so that his wife was very much exercised over my persistent hunting of them. She was continually cautioning me about exposing myself. I shot that year, as before stated, 45 grizzlies, not counting those that got away in the brush[,] most of them no doubt fatally wounded but one of my rules was never to go into a thicket if I knew it contained a bear. I killed some very large ones & one in particular whose skin I sold to one of the trading vessels[.] It was much larger than any bullock's hide they took on board.

My mode of hunting the grizzly was this. If I discovered them feeding was I rode as near as possible with my horse[,] keeping always to leeward; then dismounting I tied him with a small cord which he could easily break if the bear got after him. I then approached the bear cautiously & under cover if practicable[,] taking advantage of the ground, timber &c. I would get as near as 40 to 50 yds. & under no circumstances did I shoot at a longer distance than 100 yds. I never shot at the head as unless a ball could be put fairly in the eye or ear, the constant motion of the head when feeding & its shape make the glancing off of the bullet almost certain. The breast is a good place to aim at if the bear is facing you but I prefer the side just back of the fore shoulder. The aim should be at a spot well back of the shoulder, as a bear heart lies much farther back than that of a deer of [or] buffalo. Here a shot is almost sure to drop a bear dead in his tracks, sometimes they will run a few yards, but very rarely do they move from where they a[re] shot. One objection to shooting them in the breast is the danger of striking some of the larger bones against which the bullet flattens. On the side the ribs are the only bones & they are easily broken or even pierced by an ordinary bsized bullet. If I approached them through timber I always looked about me before firing & selected a tree that I could readily climb in case of need. When feeding they are easily approached, by keeping to leeward, as although their scent is extraordinary, they keep no watch; relying no doubt upon their poweress [prowess].

When a she bear with cubs is found, it is best to shoot the old one first & the young ones can be shot at leisure as they will not leave the mother. I never shot at a bear unless I could see him plainly & was within good range & in this way I never missed one. In a thicket a bear has one most decidedly at a disadvantage, & under no circumstances should they be followed, however severely wounded. If a person is cool, a good shot & above all prudent there is but little danger in hunting bears. Most of those I shot this year & if in fact at all times, I killed almost as easily a[s] I could a squirrel. A person who is not a good shot, cool & cautious & has a good rifle has no business to hunt bears. Nowadays with the magazine & breech-loading guns bear hunting is very tame. In my time we had only muzzle loaders & although, by carrying two guns, we had a reserve shot, our chances were poor if a wounded bear got after us. It will sometimes happen that, in passing through bushes, one comes suddenly upon a bear & there of course is [sic] his chances are poor. When a grizzly is lying in a thicket & hears noise, it keeps perfectly quiet until the animal or person is within reach. At this time there was no sale for bear skins [so] that we never took the trouble [to] skin them unless for our own [use] or to make a present to some friend or acquaintance. Sparks killed very few bears, but he got chased by one once, & had such a narrow escape that I could never prevail upon him afterward to hunt them. In fact, he became sor much afraid of them, that when we were together in a bear country, he would never leave me & often prevented me from shooting when I had a good chance at one[.]

Why a man who is a good shot should be afraid of a bear under ordinary circumstances I cannot imagine, yet my experience has been that very few even among hunters care to hunt the grizzly much. There are plenty who will talk about what they would do but they will run when it comes to the point. I think I must have killed, on this coast, at different times, upwards of 200 grizzlies. While at Branch[']s ranch I usually hunted alone, but on one trip took with me an American, a young man, green, awkward, from New England I think, who had been cast away on a whaler somewhere along the Coast, & was then in the employ of Branch as a laborer, or ranch hand. He was no more afraid of a grizzly than of a suck[l]ing calf, it was not courage however but stupidity. Having killed several deer about six miles from the Ranch, & knowing they were short of meat I decided to send my companion in with some venison.

We had made a camp from where a plain trail led to the ranch. Loading his horse with meat I started him off with the instruction to keep to the trail & to hasten forward as fast as possible in order to reach the ranch before nightfall. The next day while out hunting I found him wandering about on foot & completely lost. Shortly after leaving camp the day before he found a large tree fallen across the trail & thinking to make a short cut [?] the Ranch, he left the trail & struck out in the direction he supposed the ranch lay. Night came on & he could see no ranch. He[...] wandered about until the horse gave out, when he abandoned it & continued his search for the ranch on foot. So he continued to wander all that night & the next day nup to the time I found him. A short distance off we found the horse quietly grazing; the meat still lashed to her. How ceither of them missed being caught by grizzlies, I cannot imagine. We were in the very worst grizzly country in Cal. & the horse with fresh venison would have proved a dainty morsel for them. I asked the New Englander why he did not go around the tree & continue to follow the trail. He looked a little surprised & foolish & finally said that he might have done that but at the time it had not occurred to him. Not long after this I went out to hunt one morning, leaving him alone in camp.

Upon returning, I found a large grizzly walking leisurely around the camp, pausing occasionally to stand up & watch the New Englander who was seated whittling wholly unconscious of the presence of the bear. Even after I had shot it & told him of its nactions he did not seem in the least alarmed. We returned to the ranch a few days after this incident, & on the way I discovered a bear a [&] two yearling cubs in a small ravine with a willow thicket at its bottom. I fired from the top of the ravine & must have wounded the bear badly as she barely managed to crawl into the thicket. As soon as I fired I called out, ["]I have fixed him[,"] at the same time pointing in the direction of the willows. My New Englander who had as usual seen nothing started to run down the ravine & before I realized what [h]is intentions were had nearly reached the willows[.] I shouted to him to come back, & he turned just as the two cubs ran out at him. A moment later & he would have been torn to pieces. He came back laughing perfectly unconscious of the danger he had escaped. I never took him out with me again.
About January of 1838 or before, I returned to Santa Barbara. Soon after, I made a short hunt on [the] Islands with Black Steward & Simmonds, in which we secured 25 or 30 skins.

In April of 1838, [I] made a hunt up the coast with Sparks & Black Steward. We returned in Sept., having been gone about 5 months. We went as far as Monterey; we got about 60 skins. The following winter I laid in Santa Barbara.

In the Spring of 1839, Sparks, Hewitt, an American sailor, & I started on a hunt down on Lower Calcoast. We had 3 canoes & a large whaleboat schooner rigged of about 4 tons burthen. We went as far as Redondo Island & returned in 5 mos. with 109 otter skins, which we sold at San Diego for $37.00 a piece. We arrived at Santa Barbara in Sepet. [Sept.][,] remained on shore about 2 weeks & then went on [a] short hunt over to the Islands[.] We returned in Nov. with about 30 skins.

Soon after, I again went up to Monterey to see Alvarado about the Wasner ranch, having been to see him before on each of our previous hunts up the coast. I was still unsuccessful. While here in Monterey I went to see Graham & Nale who were raising a company to go back across the plains to the States. They urged me strongly to join them, but when I saw the few men the[y] had succeeded in mustering I refused, as I saw at the first glance that they were not the kind of men to go into the Mts. with & especially since the indians [sic] had become so hostile. Graham & Nale came to the same conclusion later & gave it up. At this time Alvarado told me that I could get no land until I married a Californian.

Returning to Santa Barbara, I fitted out for another hunt to the Lower Cal[.] coast with Sparks & Hewitt. We started in April, I think the first, of 1840. Our crew consisted of Plomer, a Dane whose name I have forgotten, Ray, an Englishman, & a Scotchman [Scotsman] whose plain complexion homely face gained for him the name of ColoradoBonito; his right name I do not now recollect. Besides these we took [on] this trip for the first time 4 Mission Inds. They took readily to paddling & soon became very useful. These I think were the first Mission Inds[.] ever employed by otter hunters on this coast, & they soon superseded the Kanakas. In after years some of the hunters began to teach a few how to shoot & that soon spoiled them as from being very docile & willing, those who learned to use the rifle soo became lazy & independent, even saucy. Our armament consisted of 8 rifles & 3 shot guns. Sparks & I had each 3 rifles & Hewitt 2. We went as far as Cerros Island & were gone about 6 months. On our return we touched in San Diego for supplies. We anchored a short distance out & Sparks [at] once went ashore to buy provisions, &c. He had hardly landed when a Mex. woman told him to look out as all Americans were being arrested & driven out of the country. This Sparks would not believe until having proceeded up town he was met by the alcalde who informed him that he had received orders to arrest him as well as all the Americans who were with him. Sparks answered that he could carry out his orders if he wished but that if arrested his companions would come up & release him by force if necessary. As the alcalde had no force there he did not offer to molest Sparks any further. Returning to the boat he told us what had happened, whereupon we unanimously agreed to resist any attempt to take us. Accordingly we carefully reloaded all [our?] guns & kept a strict watch during the next week that we laid there. The Alert was in San Diego at that time & her supercargo Alfred Robinson, confirmed to us the report that all Americans were being driven out of the country. About a week after our arrival news reached us that the vessel conveying the prisoners to San Blas was on her way to San Diego, so we dropped down below San Diego about 20 miles to a small bay & made a camp on shore, & in a position to defend ourselves. Three days after the vessel passed within a quarter of a mile of shore and, although they must have seen us, no attempt was made to take us.

Having taken in sufficient supplies at San Diego, & without disposing of our otter skins, we returned to the Lower Coast going down as far as Cajalo. At San Quintin bay we found 9 other otter hunters, 4 of them Lower Californians, Black Steward, O Brien, a man by name of Sam & some other foreigners from Los Angeles & San Diego[.] There were in all 9 canoes, none of them properly equipped & but very few good otter hunters among this whole party. They were making for a point low down on the Coast that had not been hunted for some time & that, it was known, abounded in otter. We, too, were bound to this place but not knowing the watering place where all of us must necessarily stop to fill our water barrels, we determined to keep them in sight, as we knew them to be well acquainted with the Coast. They seemed to be as determined that we should not keep in sight of them & when we started along down the Coast together, tried several times without success to leave us behind. At Cerros Island we made a slight stay which they took advantage of & pulled direct for the water, we followed close after them & passed so close to [them] that, but for a dense fog, [we] would have seen them. Neither of us saw each other, however, & we continued along down the Coast for several miles to a watering place with which [we] were acquainted & here took in a supply. We lost no time in getting under way again & by a little effort reached the hunting grounds first. Before the others arrived, we had already killed 17 otter & had their skins staked out on shore. At the sight of these the other party were not a little chagrined, but declared they would get ahead of us yet, & so proceeded farther down the Coast until they got beyond the otter grounds entirely. We did not see them again until we returned to San Diego. We had been gone altogether 7 months & secured 153 skins. We arrived in Santa Barbara in Nov. 1840.
Here I remained this winter & on February 13, (1841) was married to Miss Sinforosa Sanchez, my present wife.

In April following Capt. Wilson & Burton fitted out a Hermaphrodite brig, the property of Wilson & Scott, to hunt first up the coast & then returning to go down on the Lower Coast. Wilson took charge of the brig & furnished the hunters with everything & took half of the skins. There were as hunters besides myself & Burton, Breck & Hewitt. We went up to just below Monterey & returned, being gone about 2 1/2 months. Both Wilson & Burton were very close, not half-feeding our men, so that those in my boat frequently refused to work, giving as an excuse that they were hungry. This did not suit me, so I left them as soon as we got back here from Monterey. They continued south to Cerros Islan[d] returning to Santa Barbara in July 1841. Upon arriving here or soon thereafter, Sparks & I made a hunt on the Islands, returning in Oct. with some 20 to 30 otter skins.

In [the] fail of 1840 I bought bo what is now known as the Burton Mound property from Joseph Chapman, who had purchased it from the Mission. It had formerly been used by to stow hides in by the Fathers[.] In I remained on shore until spring of 1843. In the spring of this year Thompson (A. B.) & Jones fitted out their Brig "Bolivar" for a six mos. hunt to Leeward. There were six hunters, Dye, one of our original Ark. company, Dawson an American, Sparks, Hewitt, José a Cal. indian [Indian], & myself. We were gone barely two months, owing to our provisions giving out. Coming back we were put on allowance & suffered not a little with hunger before we reached Santa Barbara.

After this trip I remained on shore until 1845. In April of this year, Sparks, Breck & I as hunters with a sufficient crew, made a trip to the Leeward. We were taken down in the "Bolivar" & landed on the Cerros Island together with lumber to build a boat. We hunted around Cerros Island some & then Breck & I went over to Morro Hermoso, Sparks & two of our crew being left to build the boat. The "Bolivar" had left us & gone down to Mazatlan, where she was sold[.] our [sic] new boat was to be about 4 tons burthen & was the property of Thompson and Jones, who furnished the material for building her. The boat being finished, all hands went over to Morro Hermoso. Here & among the neighboring islands we continued to hunt until our return. At Morro Hermoso we found a large school of otter, killing about 85 of them. All together we got 105 skins. We had expected to hunt some below San Diego on our return but were unable to get supplies at that Port.

During the following winter we remained on shore. In Nov. of this winter (1845), Micheltorena, with a force of about 100 men, passed through here for Los Angeles. They tried to get Sparks, Breck, & me to join them, but we did not care to. Sutter was in command of foreigners, of which there were some 30. Gant, Graham, Knight (their pilot), Merrit & others in the party I was acquainted with. They stayed here a few days. There were about 15 of the foreigners who acted as an advance guard. I was acquainted with several of them & having noticed that they were careless I advised them to keep a sharp lookout & not take any chances.
They paid no heed to this, however, & just this side of San Buenaventura while riding leisurely along smoking & talking they were surrounded & captured, not one of them escaping.

I was told that upon Micheltorena nearing San Buenaventura, Castro retreated to Los Angeles, while the former continued to advance until near that city. Here the two forces met, when the foreigners of the lower party[,] Workman, Rowland, & others, had a talk with the foreigners of Micheltorena's party & they came to the conclusion that they would let the Californians fight it out among themselves. As a matter of course there was no fighting, it being settled by Micheltorena leaving the country.

In March 1846 Thompson wanted us to make a trip up the coast & fitted us out with a few supplies, telling us that we could get more at Monterey on his credit. Besides me, there was Fife & a man by name of McCoy. McCoy had been mate of a whaler that was cast away at Monterey. He took passage for Chile in the Famer, Capt. Thompson's ship, & was again cast away. The Famer & the Vandalia a Boston ship were both caught in a South Easter off Santa Barbara. The Vandalia barely escaped, while the Famer went ashore at Goleta just above Santa Barbara. Her crew were saved, but had she struck a ship's length farther above[,] every man would have perished. The Capt. of the Famer, Nye, was ashore in Santa Barbara at the time.

Charley Brown was also with us on this trip in charge of the large boat[.] he [sic] had made his first trip with us the year before to Cerros Island, & took charge of the boat we built at that time. He was a Prussian, had been a sailor, having come here from the Sandwich Island[s] with Capt[.] Wilson, & was one of the most trustworthy & steady men we ever had with us.
Arrived at Monterey we found that we could get nothing on Thompson's credit, so we paid for our supplies & hunted up the coast as far as Fort Ross, returning in Nov. (1846) with 90 skins.

On this trip, while lying at Tamales Bay, Fife, Charley Brown, McCoy, & myself started out one day after deer; we took of the inds. [sic], called Blas with us. McCoy & the indian [Indian] had remained some distance in our rear, on a ridge along which we were passing; all on foot. We had left them but a short time when the indian [Indian] came running up to us saying that a bear was killing McCoy. We hastened back & found McCoy lying on the ground & very badly hurt. He was bitten in the legs & back, & had some of the cords of one of this thighs literally torn out. I dressed his wounds & wrapped them up, applying to them a wild herb, whose name I have forgotten; it resembles very much our tansy. The ind. [Ind.] we had sent [on] to camp for the rest of the men who soon arrived with oars, with which we formed a litter & carried him to camp. The next day we took him to Bodega. Having sent word ahead Capt[.] Smith very kindly came out to meet us. We left McCoy in charge of Capt[.] Smith's mother[-]in[-]law, a Peruvian lady & in less than six weeks he was able to ride his a horse to San Francisco. ThHe afterwards told us that he was walking along with the ind. [Indian] when they came suddenly upon a she bear with cubs. The bear no sooner saw them than with ears back & mouth open, she rushed for McCoy, who had barely time to fire whe[n] she was on him. The bear knocked him down & made the wounds before mentioned. His shot had not struck her, at least there were no signs of blood, for he broke his gun stock over her head when she came up, which of course only infuriated her. Her cubs began crying & she ran to them or it might have been still worse with McCoy. The first trip Sparks[,] Simmonds, Black Steward & I made up the coast, Black Steward had a similar adventure with a bear[.] We were all out hunting & became separated. Black Steward wounded a deer & got of[f] his horse to crawl up on it & finish it. I[n] passing through a clump of bushes a small she bear jumped on him, & before he could defend himself knocked him down. He was a strong, powerful man, & immediately grappled with the bear. They rolled over & over for several yards, the bear biting him very severely in the several places, & tearing his coat in pieces.

Watching a favorable opportunity, he attempted to draw his knife, when the bear bit him in the fore arm tearing out some of the cords. At this moment hearing her cubs cry she raised up on her hind legs when Black Steward seized his gun & shot her[,] breaking her back. He did not stop to finish her but made for camp. The bite in his arm was very bad, & he was scratched & bruised all over. Sparks went to the place next day & finding the bear sitill [still] alive killed her. He said that the bushes for a space of over ten yards square the bushes were broken down & the ground torn up where Black Steward had fought with the grizzly.

On this last trip (1846) when we passed Monterey the Americans had already taken it. We were just outside when the Eng. Man of War entered & we could plainly hear their salutes that were fired. We stayed at Monterey two or three days for supplies. Upon our return we also made a few days' stay there, & found Fremont there. He asked me if I would join him & I promised to do so as soon as we reached Santa Barbara, & I said I thought that Sparks would join him also. Here of course we heard that the Californians had risen & retaken the Lower Country but we supposed, having heard nothing to the contrary, that everything was quiet in Santa Barbara.

Arriving near the port of San Luis Obispo, we landed just above the point, & from the bluff above could plainly see the houses. There seemed to be something unusual going on so we concluded not to venture any nearer. Scott & Wilson's ship was lying in the Harbor & I at one time thought of going down to her, but I finally decided not to go near them as from their ill feeling towards Americans I could not expect any protection in case of need. We had hardly landed when Breck[,] whose ranch was close by[,], came down to put us on our guard against a party of Totoi Pico's men that were coming out to capture us that night.

We first decided to make a fight & accordingly selected a place easily defended. We buried our skins, some 75, provisions & extra ammunition in the sand, & determined that if closely pushed we three would take one of our canoes which was hauled up ready for use & with our skins put off out [of?] their reach as we had no doubt that our skins was [sic] what they wanted most. Our large boat with the rest of our crew was some distance from shore. Fife, Charley Brown & I were on shore. All the foregoing preparations were made after supper. As soon as it got dark we proceeded to our position on a high bank overlooking & completely commanding the only approach to our camp. We drew lots as to who should stand guard the first three hours. It fell to my lot although I had hoped that I might get the first part of the night for sleeping. Fife & Brown layid down but continued to talk until I suggested that if they did not care to sleep they had better stand guard & let me lie down. They immediately got up & I lay down, but found it impossible to sleep. About midnight having concluded that we were not likely to be attacked, I suggested that we continue our voyage. We accordingly got under way & continued down the Coast the rest of that night & all the next day the weather being too rough to allow of us to hunt. Before night the weather grew worse & the wind blew fearfully. Night came on [...]so dark that we could hardly see a boat[']s length. The wind, a North Wester, increased in fury until we had to run before it. We tied our boats together & some of the men tried to sleep as all of us were nigh worn out. Since starting we had been able to cook no food & we were all wet to the skin. The sea was running mountains high[,] the waves breaking at least a half a mile off the shore. In the morning we rounded Point Concepcion & the change was wonderful; as south of the point the sea was quite smooth. We had decided to take advantage of the first opportunity to land & make some coffee, &c but being anxious to arrive home we concluded to keep in [on] since we were so near. We arrived early in the day. We had hardly landed when a party of Californians came down & arrested us & took us before the Alcalde Anto. Ma. Ortega & Raymundo Carrillo.

We were examined separately as to our movements, &c during our absence from Santa Barbara. Brown & Fife were allowed to go free because they were foreigners. I was retained a prisoner because I was an American. I told them that they had better leave me unmolested, but they said I must go to Los Angeles. They permitted me to sleep at Sparks' house that night[,] he being responsible for me. The next day they made me pay $200, on my skins, an arbitrary duty, as I had already paid for my license. Capt. D de la Guerra was the only person in the place who had money & from him I obtained the $200, but he compelled me to leave him as security 15 otter skins. I was then allowed to go to my house, & get ready to go the next morning to Los Angeles. I told them I wanted a horse & saddle but they said I had money & could buy one, & that they would give me but a few hours in which to do it. Arriving at home my wife told me that they were searching all houses for arms, that Raymundo Carrillo & Franco de la Guerra had with a party of men, visited the house but a few days before taking a shot gun 12 lbs. of powder & a quantity of caps, shot, & balls. I immediately hid two of my rifles & a pistol with the powder I had brought back, but I had no balls. I purposely left in sight my third rifle, not of much account. That same evening the same party surrounded the house, made a search for arms & took my rifle. They also rearrested me. I was allowed to go however upon promising that I would go to Los Angeles about with my father-in-law, or at least as far as San Buenaventura & then [on] alone. They gave me a passport at my request, as [sic] having taking [taken] everything from me[,] even my pocket knife, I told them I would need it on the road to protect myself. Having reached my house, I set to work to clean one of my rifles & my pistol & run bullets. As soon as it was dark I started off on foot up the Coast to meet Fremont. The night was very dark so that I left the town without being observed. That night I reached Dos Pueblos about 18 miles from here. Here I hid in a ravine near the road determined to shoot the first Californian who should come in sight, & take his horse. A short distance from me was a band of horses, & early in the morning I saw a horseman coming out towards them. When he got near enough I recognized him as Vicente Ortega one of the best Californians & men in those times & I had not the heart to shoot him although so mad was I at the treatment I had received that I would not have hesitated if any other man had come within range. I remained there another night & the third day returned to my house along the beach.

There were three or four Californian women at my house besides my wife & they at all times knew of my movements but they never offered to betray me, but on the contrary kept me informed of what was going on among their countrymen. I stopped at my house all night & early the next morning went over to the foothills near Montecito, about 2 or 3 miles from town, returning to my house at night. A day or two after my return from Dos Pueblos I was told that a small party had arrived from above, escorting Thos. O. Larkin whom they were taking a prisoner to Los Angeles. Larkin had escaped from Monterrey but he was retaken. I was told to keep out of the way for a day or two as, news having come from below of my nonarrival at Los Angeles[,] they would probably make a vigorous search for me. I remained one night in the foot hills, the next day, having taken a position on the top of a rise from which I could plainly see the road. I staid there all day waiting for the party with Larkin to pass. Sometime in the forenoon a Californian by the name of Juarez was riding along a short distance from me when he caught sight of me & having recognized me, called out for me to come to him. To his summons I made no answer & did not move from my position. He then said that if I did not come to where he was he would have to come for me. To this I paid no attention & he rode towards me. Nearby there were bushes & into these I got & waited for him. He came up to within about 30 or 40 yds. steps & turned about & went away. Had he approached me a yds steps farther he would have been a dead man. He reported having seen me to the people in town & a party of 5 or 6 men was immediately formed & came out after me. I saw them riding about but they did not come near where I was. I am inclined to think that they did not want to find me very much. There were but few of them that did not fear me. For some time after that I continued to visit my house almost invariably sleeping there & sometimes not leaving it for several days at a time. Our house was in [on[ the top of a slight rise from which we had a clear view of everything as on on all sides of us for several hundred yards. There were no trees near it then. We had a small mill for grinding our wheat the motive power being a[n] old lame horse. This horse had been taken a number of times by the Califs, but was always returned when they found it unserviceable. As soon as it grew dark I was in the habit of taking this horse & picketing him out some distance from the house where I let him feed until late at night. I began to get careless & one evening while taking him out earlier than usual, I was seen by a Cal[i]f[.] woman by the name of Vasquez, who immediately reported it to Capt. de la Guerra who was even a[t] that time like a king in authority here. A party of 12 men was raised, headed by Guillermo Carrillo, Raymundo Carrillo, & Guillermo Hartnell, Jr. The[y] came to my house about 10 o'clock at night[,] surrounded it & demanded to know if I was there. We were all asleep when they came but were soon awakened by the noise[.] My wife answered that I was in Los Angeles. They ordered her to get a light at once & admit them[.]

This was done after considerable delyay during which hid myself. In one side of the thick adobe wall there had once been a door, but which having been closed on the outside of the wall left a large space in which two or three persons could stand with ease. Against this & completely concealing it was a large wooden cupboard. One might have lived there for years without discovering its existence unless the cupboard should be moved. Having entered[,] the Californians made as they supposed a thorough search of the house & not content with having looked into every place once they repeated it. Above the room where I had been sleeping was a loft, which they ascended to several times. Our bake ovens[,] one of which was in the house[,] received their careful attention, trunks, boxes, matrasses [mattresses] & in short everything was rigidly examined. The search being ended they apologized to my wife for the intrusion & withdrew. I exercised a little more caution for the next three days at the end of which time Fremont arrived. He had hardly got into camp when I went up to see him. I told him how I had been treated & he ordered a Lieut[.] & several men to accompany me for the purpose of searching whatever houses I suspected as containing arms &c. As a matter of course I led them first to the de la Guerra house. Here we found the Capt.[,] his son Anto[.] Ma. & Cesaer [Caesar] Lataillade, standing in the porch surrounded by several members & servants of the family.

I addressed the Capt. saying that as they had searched my house to their hearts['] content but a few days before, I had come to return the favor. He flushed up a little, but soon regaining his composure, he said in his pompous & authoritative way that it could not be done. This answer I communicated to the Lieut. accompanying me who at once instructed me to say to the gentleman that if every door, box[,] trunk, &c in the house were not opened immediately they would be broken open.

The tone & manner of the officer were not to be mistaken & the Capt., not without some grumbling ordered his son Anto. Ma., to open everything, at the same time handing him a large bunch of keys. Lataillade made some insolent remarks which as soon as uttered I interpreted to the Lieut. ["]Tell him to shut up at once[,"] said the officer. I did so, & Mr. Lataillade subsided. One of the soldiers was detailed to examine the contents of the trunks &c. under the supervision of the Lieut. This soldier said he saw a large quantity of gold & silver coin in the trunks here[.] wWe found no arms, ammunition or saddles, &c. The de la Guerra's [sic] [have] never forgave me for thus humiliating them & would [have] undoubtedly had me put out of the way afterwards had they not feared me, for long after the Americans came they continued to control if not rule the native population[.]

I asked Sparks if he would join Fremont & he refused. He said that he was a Mexican citizen & could not do it. This answer of Sparks I conveyed to Fremont who said nothing but looked very much displeased. Shortly after I overheard the men discussing the subject & it was quite evident that the proposal of some of them to raze Sparks['] house & confiscate his stock among which was a fine race mare, would be adopted & probably carried out that very night. I lost no time in apprising Sparks of their intentions & he at once went to tell Fremont that he would join him.

We afterwards learned that a party of Totoi Pico's men, under an Italian cutthroat called Antonio, had visited our camp at [St Louis Obp ?] shortly after we vacated it, as they found our fires still burning. Just before reaching our camp they had halted & one of their men struck a light for the purpose of smoking his cigarrete. They supposed that we were on the lookout, saw this light & hurriedly departed & attributed to this man's carelessness the failure of the project. Some of them frankly admitted afterwards that they intended to kill all of us & take our skins, &c. Antonio their leader was afterwards killed in the skirmish at Salinas when Capt[.] Burrows & party were cut off.

While at Monterey we were camped on a wooded point just above the town. Here we were visited by the American officers from the "Savannah" & "Congress" & the English officers from the "Collingwood." Our camp was quite an object of interest to them, especially the Eng. officers, for it was about the same as we used in the Mountains. On shore too most of us dressed in buckskin. They shot with us at a target with our rifles, but there were no very good shots among them. I talked with the Eng. officers about the American occupation of Cal. & they invariably expressed themselves as pleased with the change, "because," they said, "California will now be worth something".

Both Commodore Stockton & Genl. Fremont asked me very many questions about this section & each was anxious for me to accompany them. I told them that my present circumstances would not allow me to leave the men with me before reaching Santa Barbara, but promised Fremont that I would join him at that place. From our camp I very frequently went into town for supplies & also to have a talk with some of Fremont's men who were old acquaintances of mine, one of them being the brother of Alex Sinclair. Happening in town one afternoon I found them shooting at a target[.] The Eng[.] & Amer. officers had been invited by Fremont to witness the shooting of some of his most skillful marksman [marksmen]. From 15 to 20 of his best shots had been selected for the purpose. A target was set up at from 70 to 80 yards. A small piece of white paper about three inches square with a small bullseye in the middle was the center of the target. I was standing nearby the men watching the shooting when Fremont asked if I would not like to shoot. I said that I would shoot if he wished it. A man by the name of Martin, an acquaintance, & now living near Gilroy (& I understa[n]d is now blind) was there & had a fine new rifle. This he urged me to take[,] having brought none with me. I fired three shots & put all three of my bullets in the paper & close together. One of Fremont[']s men did the same, but the remainder of them although putting every shot very close to the paper when they did not hit it, failed to get all three within the paper. While here in Santa Barbara Fremont was camped just back of the "Sisters['] College." He staid here about a week. The first day we moved down to Carpinteria where we remained all night. The next day we got into San Buenaventura early in the afternoon & went into camp on the South side of the river.

Shortly after we arrived, our scouts came in & reported a body of the enemy's calvalry [cavalry] approaching from the direction of the Santa Clara river. We immediately marched out beyond the Mission & formed. The enemy soon appeared to the number of about 80 on a low ridge but a short distance away. A field piece was run out & two shots fired at them. At the [sic] second shot scattered them & they were soon out of sight. Several of us wanted to follow them but Fremont would not allow us to scatter. Thise next day we moved up along the Sta. Clara River. Soon after starting we saw numbers of the enemy's horsemen, ahead of us, but scattered & they continued in sight until we [reached?] Saticoy when they crossed the river a short distance above us. During the day a Truckee indian [Indian] belonging to our party got off some distance from the clolum[n] when a few of the enemy made a dash for him, but he succeeded in getting back to the column. That night we camped just above Saticoy on the N[.] side of the river. The following morning we continued our march keeping along the river & catching an occasional glance of small bodies of the enemy. They kept at a considerable distance, apparently watching our movements. In the afternoon we camped at the San Francisquito Ranch on the S[.] side of [the] river. That evening by order of Fremont all arms were discharged, cleaned & reloaded, as we did not know how soon the enemy might attack us. The next day we followed the San Fernando trail until we reached the foot of the mountains. Small parties of horsemen[,] probably reconoitring [reconnoitering] parties, appeared in our front from time to time as on the previous day. We camped at the foot of the mountain somewhere about 3 or 4 P. M. Upon our arrival, about 80 of the enemy appeared on the ridge in our front & spread themselves along its top evidently with the object of making a show of their forces.

Here they began to throw up [breast?] works, & as they had l ft [left] word at San Buenaventura that we should not pass the Cuesta, we expected to have some trouble with them here. That night the guard was doubled but nothing occurred to disturb us. The next morning about 8 o'clock 200 men were ordered to dismount & dividing[,] advance up the ravines on either side to the summit of the ridge, & surround the enemy. Thi[s] was executed without opposition & upon reaching the top of the Cuesta we found the enemy gone. We took up our line of march again[,] still following the trail. A short distance beyond the Cuesta a tempery [temporary] excitement was caused by the sight of men among the neighboring timber, but it was soon over when our advance found them to be indian [Indian] woodchoppers from the San Fernando Mission. We reached this Mission about noon. Here we found a foreigner with a letter from Stockton to Fremont, awaiting us. Not liking his looks Fremont ordered him to be retained as a prisoner. Commodore Stockton informed Fremont of his arrival & occupation of Los Angeles, & offered to send him reinforcements if needed. We stayed at the Mission that day & they [sic] following one marched to Cahuenga, where the treaty was signed & thence to Los Angeles. Just before we arrived at Los Angeles, we were met by Andres Pico, who presented Fremont with one of his finest horses.

We remained at Los Angeles about 10 days when[,] having been discharged[,] Sparks & I returned home, arriving in Santa Ba[r]bara about the last of Jan. 1847. That Spring the volunteers from N. Y. arrived here & after camping near the beach for some time, removed the [to] the Saint Charles Hotel. Soon after two companies of them were sent South.

I remained on shore until Mar. 1848, when Sparks, McCoy, Fife & I fitted out for a trip on the coast N. of S. F. We shipped our canoes, &c., from here to S. F. There we built a 5 ton boat which was placed in charge of Charley Brown. In May (1848) we started & hunted up as far as Cape Mendocino. Twelve miles above San Francisco we found a few otter & a few mor[e] in Drakes Bay, but above this for a long distance there are not otters. On the S. side of Cape Mendocino we ran ashore & camped for the purpose of killing meat. The indians [sic] having discovered us soon began to collect until there were from 300 to 400 camped but a short distance from us, most of them were armed with clubs & bows & arrows. Several of them tried to approach camp, but we motioned to them to keep away & as they saw that we had our guns in our hadsnds they went away[.]

We built a small fort of drift wood to protect ourselves. About this time it began to bolow so strong that it was almost impossible to keep one's feet. The wind continued for 3 or 4 days. During the stormblow the men began to get frightened about the indians [sic] & so we decided to leave as soon as the wind stopped. It fortunately stopped at midnight & we lost no time in getting away, as we were certain that the indians [sic] would attack as so[o]n as the blow was over; it being impossible for them to shoot their arrows straight in a strong wind. Farther down the Coast we again landed & finished getting a supply of meat.

We next landed at Fort Ross & here for the first time we heard of the gold diggings. The prospect of getting $16 a day when their monthly wages barely amounted to that was too great a temptation for our men, who insisted on leaving us at S. F. Arrived at this latter place we sold off our skins (some 20) & paid our men. We then proceeded up the Sac. River & allowed our men to go with us as far as they wished.

At Yuba we left our large boat & with our small boats proceeded up the Feather River. Here we separrated [separated] after dividing our supplies, of coffee, sugar, &c. that we had brought with us, & each man began working on his own account. I had brought with me from Santa Barbara a Mission indian [Indian][.] He had been with me already 2 years, & he now staid by me. He was very sober and industrious. Our claim yielded us about 15 ounces a day. It was a very rich claim however, the rest of our companions whose claims adjoined ours did not do as well. So intense was the heat that the 4th day every man was down with the feover. I was the first to recover & was obliged to look after the others. As soon as possible we moved farther down the river. Here my indian [Indian] died. We then moved still farther down the river, when O Brien died. This decided us to leave the diggings, & besides Sparks was still quite sick. At S. F. we took passage in a sailing vessel for Sta. Barbara, paying each $70 for our passage. The vessel was a trader & came very slowly down the Coast [,] this enabled me to do some considerable hunting & I kept our table supplied with game, such as wild geese & ducks, &c. & in consideration of this contribution the Supercargo deducted $20 from my fare.

About the last of March 1849 I again started for the mines with Davis[,] a nNorweigen [Norwegian], & several natives of this place. I think there were 15 of us all together. Before reaching San Luis Obispo, we were joined on the road by a surgeon who had been under Taylor in Mexico. At San Luis Obp. Burton joined us. We went to the San Joaquin River where we were joined by Capt[.] Walker & 3 or 2 men, who accompanied us as far as Stockton. From here we went to the mines between the Tuolom[m]e& Stonislau [Stanislaus] rivers. Here Burton left us. I staid in this neighborhood all summer, prospecting considerable, working many claims, but none of them rich. This section was litterally [literally] alive with people. I The following winter I went down near Knight[']s Ferry & hunted deer for about 2 months. I sold them for an average of $10, a piece, & made about $400 more than I had made the whole season at mining. Near this same Ferry I staked of[f]a piece of land & laid the foundation for a house, intending to return soon and settle there.

I came back to Santa Barbara in Jan. 1850. So memany reports reached me here of the numbers of people dying where I intended to settle that I gave it up & never went back ther[e]. This same month (Jan[.] 1850,) I sold my house & place to Hinchman. I then went to S. F. & bought a small schooner of 17 tons burthen, with which I returned to Santa Barbara & in that & in the following year of 1851 made a few short hunts for otter. In the latter part of 1850 Capt. Alden with the "Quickstep" came here to survey around among the Islands. He employed me as pilot for about two weeks, paying me $10 a day.

Soon after buying my schr. in S. F. I bought out the interest of a man by the name of Bruce who had an interest sheep on the San Miguel Island. In Feb.y following I took 45 head of sheep & 20 17 head of cattle over to the Ilsland. I had a few sheep but was obliged to buy some to make up the number for which I paid $10 apiece. I kept this Island about 17 years. From my original stock [of] 45 sheep, 17 cattle, 2 hogs & 7 horses[, ]I had in 1862[,] 6000 sheep, 200 cattle, 100 hogs & 32 horses. In the drought of 1863–4I lost 5000 sheep[,] 180 cattle, a few hogs, & 30 horses.

In 18665-6, Mr[.] Chaffee of San Buenaventura offered me $10000 for the Island [,] but finding that there was no purchaser to be found who would pay him an advance of from $2000 to $3000 on this sum he backed out. I afterward in 18670 sold it to the Mills Bros. for $10,000. I had no desire to dispose of the Island, but my sons persuaded me to do so as they had become tired of living there. I have not been to the Island for several years but I am told that [it] is almost covered with sand[.]

In April of 1852 I went over to the Islands with my schr. accompanied by a foreigner by [the] name Tom Jefferies, who is still living here, & 2 indians [sic], for sea gulls' eggs. These eggs were in great demand at that time. We went direct to the San Nicolas & having arrived early in the day, Jefferies, one of the indians [sic], & I landed & travelled along the beach towards the upper end of the Island some 6 or 7 miles. At a short distance from the beach, about 200 yds. we discovered the footprints of a human being, probably of a woman[,] as they were quite small. They had evidently been made during the previous rainny [rainy] season as they were well defined & sunk quite deep into the soil then soft but now dry & hard. At a distance of a few hundred yds. back from the beach & about 2 miles apart we found 3 [?] small circular enclosures, made of sage brush. Their thin walls [were] perhaps 5 feet high. And the whole enclosure 6 feet in diameter, & with a small narrow[,] opening on one side. We examined them carefully, but found nothing that would indicate their having been occupied for a long time[,] as the grass was growing within them. They all occupied slight rises of ground[.] Outside of the huts, however, we found signs of the place having been visited not many month[s] before. Around each hut & a short distance from it were several stak[e]s or poles, usually from 4 to 6, some 7 or 8 feet high, which were standing upright in the ground, & pieces of seal blubber stuck on the top of each. The blubber was already dry, but I do not think it could have been there more than 3 or 4 mos. We had come on shore early in the morning & having found these signs of the existence of some person on the Island, we intended searching further, but a N. Wester sprang up about 10 am so that we were obligged to hasten back to the vessel.

We had seen enough to convince us of the existence of some human being on this Island who in all probability must be the indian [Indian] woman of whom Sparks had so often spoken. The gale that had suddenly sprung [up] increased in violence until we feared it would carry us out to sea. Our anchor began dragging & we were obliged to improvise one by filling a large sack with stones. We were lying on the S. side of the Island & were in a measure protected from the wind, but still ist blew very strong even there, & the swell was very great[.]

The gale continued for about 8 days, at the end of which time we ran over to the Santa Barbara Island but had no better succes[s] in finding eggs than at the San Nicolas. Here also we found several schr's at anchor that had come with the same object as we. We remained here but one night & then returned direct to Santa Barbara, our trip having been entirely profitless. Soon after I made a trip to Santa Cruz for lumber. The following winter I fitted out for another trip to the S[a]n Nicolas Island. On our former visit I had seen plenty of otter, & besides I knew that they must be abundant as they had not been hunted for years. This time Charley Brown was with me. Upon my return from my first trip I told several persons that we had seen footprints, &c. on the Island & Father Gonzalez of the Mission, having heard of it, requested me to make all possible search for her. Arriving at the Island Charley & I, with two inds. [sic] went ashore. We landed near the lower end of the Island & as I and Jeffries had done we proceeded along the beach towards the head of the Island leaving our indians [sic] in charge of the boat.

At the head of the Island I sat down to rest & Charley went around the point & some distance down the other side. When he returned he told me that he had seen fresh foot prints leading from the beach up to the high ridge which forms the head of the Island. He had followed them from the beach up over the high bank but beyond this they disappeared[,] the ground being covered with a species of moss. At one place he saw where she had apparently sat down to rest, & a small piece of drift wood lying near which had no doubt fallen from a bundle of wood shehad was carrying from the beach for her fire. I was at first in[c]lined to think that our indians [sic] had wandered off & he it was their footprints he had seen, but a moment[']s reflection showed me that it was impossible. On our way up we had also seen 7 or 8 wild dogs about a[s] large a[s] a coyote & resembling one in appearance, texcept that they were of a black & white color. They ran away as soon as they saw us so that we could not get within range of them. I was afraid these dogs had eaten the woman as we had found nothing of her. In coming up along the beach we passed a low[,] sandy stretch about a half mile wide that extends across the Island some distance below its head. Here we found some high bushes called by the natives malva real, & in the crotch of one of these a basket made of grass & covered with a piece of seal skin. tTaking it down & uncovering it we found it to contain several skins of the shag, cut square, a long sinew rop[e] as neatly & evenly twisted as any common rope, some bone needles &c. These I proposed carefully replacing, but upon a second thought scattered them about & threw the basket on the ground. Charley protested against this proceeding but he was satisfied when I explained to him, that if they were replaced in the basket by our next visit, we might be sure the woman was alive. We returned on board in the afternoon & the next day continued o[u]r search without finding anything more. We then began hunting otter which we found very thick. The 3rd or 4th day, however, a South Easter sprang up & after 6 or 7 days finding that it still continued & the sea so becoming very rough, we ran over to San Miguel where we found a good harbor. Here we laid until the gale was over when we returned to Santa Barbara. We remained on shore during the rest of the winter.

In May of 1853 I fitted out my schr. for a trip to Turtle Bay to prospect for gold. Mr[.] Forbush of this place had been told by Capt. Barney[,] an old whaler[,] that there was gold quartz thereabouts, & being convinced that there was something to be made persuaded me to make the trip. Beside[s] we us two there were Charley Brown, Tom Jefferies, & 4 Mission indians [sic]. We found a very few small pieces of quartz with the least sprinkling of gold but nothing more so we gave it up, returning to Santa Barbara in July, having killed some 15 or 20 otters on our way back.

Ten days after our return I again fitted out for a thorough hunt among the Islands, & principally around the San Nicolas. Charley Brown accompanied me as hunter, & an Irishman whom we called Colorado from his florid complexion, with three Mission indians [sic] manned our boats[,] while a fourth Mission indian [Indian] acted as cook. We reached the San Nicolas early in they [sic] day & at once went ashore for the purpose of selecting a camping place as we intended to make a stay of at least two or three months. We landed about the middle of the Island on the N. W. side & went up towards the head of the Island. A high[,] rocky bank ran along the edge of the water[,] its base for the most part being washed by the sea. A few short stretches of sanydy beach occurred here & there but they were not always accessible from the bank. About [a] 1/2 mile from the head of the Island we found a good spring of water just above the edge of the beach & in the wet soil surrounding it more footprints that must have been made but a short time before. As it was already late & we were some 6 or 7 miles from the schr. we were obliged to return without farther search, determining however to make a thorough exploration of the Island on the following day.

Accordingly the next morning early as soon as we had breakfast, all hands but the cook went on shore, at the same place where we had landed the day before. Having on our previous visits seen most of the latest signs near the head of the Island, & besides, there being but few springs in the middle & lower portion of the Island, we decided to search first from about the middle up towards then andhead. The four men struck across the low sandy stretch before mentioned, & found the basket & its contents carefully replaced in the crotch of the bush in [...] which we had first discovered it. Charl[e]y & I struck up towards the head of the Island. Having become tired, I sat downto rest and, Charley continued around the head of the Island. Reaching the place where he had seen the footprints the day before he followed [them] up the ridge. Near its top he found several huts made of whale's ribs & covered with brush. Although it was so long since they had been occupied that they were open in [on] all sides & grass was quite high within. Looking about in all directions from this point, he discovered at a distance, along the ridge, a small black object about the size of a crow which appeared to be in motion. Advancing cautiously towards it, he soon discovered it to be the indian [Indian] woman her head & shoulders only visible above one of the a [sic] small inclosures resembling those we had before discovered. He approached as near as he dared & then, raising his hat on his ramrod, signalled to the men who were then recrossing the low sandy stretch, & were plainly visible from this point.

They saw the signals, & came towards him. In the mean time, the old woman was busily employed in stripping the blubber from a piece of seal skin which she held across one knee, using in the operation a rude knife made from a piece of iron hoop stuck into a piece of rough wood for a handle. She kept up a continual jabbering to herself & every few moments would stop & look in the direction of our men, whom she had evidently been watching, her hand placed over her eyes to shade them from the sun.

Upon my his first approach there were some dogs near, which began to growl. These the old woman sent away with a yell but without looking in the direction of Charley. The men having come up, they quietly surrounded her to prevent any attempt at escape.

This being done, Charley stepped around in front of her when instead of showing any alarm she smiled & bowed[,] chattering away to them in a language wholley [wholly] unintelligible to all of them[,] even to the indians [Indians.] They seated themselves around her after having made signs to me to come up. I at first did not care to go to where they were as I supposed that they had simply discovered something that excited their curiosity & I would hear about it when they should come down. They continued to make signs to me to come there, however, so I went up & found them seated around the old woman.
She smiled and bowed to me also, & having tak
ing

en a seat she took some roots of two different kinds, one called corcomites & the name of the other I do not know, & placed them in the fire which was burning within the inclosure. As soon as they were roasted she invited us all to eat some.

The site of the inclosure or hut where we found her was on the N. W. side & near the top of the ridge that forms the upper end of the Island[;] it was not far from the best springs of water, near to the best point for fish & seal, & it commanded a good view of the greater portion of the Island. Just outside the inclosure or wind break, as I should call it, was a large pile of ashes & another of bones, showing that this had been her abode for a long time. Nearby were several stakes with blubber on them, as we had seen around the others inclosures [sic]. There was blubber also hanging on a sinew rope, similar to the one already described, which was stretched between two stakes. Near the inclosure were several baskets—some in process of construction, also two bottle shaped vessels; that for holding water; these as well of [as] the baskets being woven, & of some species of grass very common on the Island. There were also several other articles [such] as fishhooks made of bone & needles of the same material, lines or cords of sinews for fishing & the larger rope of sinews she no doubt used for snaring seals on the rocks where they came to sleep. The old woman was of medium height, but rather thick. She must have been about 50 yrs. old, but she was still strong & active. Her face was pleasing, as she was continually smiling. Her teeth were entire but worn to the gums, the effect no doubt, of eatthatting the dried seal blubber. Her head which had that app evidently been for years without any that covering protection, was covered with thick matted hair, that was once black no doubt but now it had become of a dull brown color. Her that dress clothing consisted of but a single garment of the skins of the shag, made in the form of a gown. It fitted close at the neck, had no sleeves, was girded at the waist with a sinew cord, & reached nearly to the feet. She had another dress of the same material & make in one of the baskets. These were sewed with sinews, the needles used being of bone. This place was undoubtedly where she usually lived, but in the rainy season she lived in a cave nearby. Having been requested by the Fathers at the Mission of Santa Barbara, to bring her off in case we found her, I asked the indians [sic] if they thought she could be taken by force if necessary. They thought she could. Charley Brown was of the opinion that no force would be necessary in taking her. I thereupon made signs to her to go with us but she stared at me seemingly without comprehending what was wanted. Charley then placed his hand on her shoulder to call her attention & then went through the motions of putting her things in baskets & then these on his back, at the conclusion of which he said vamoose. This she understood without any difficulty, for she at once began putting her things into her baskets. Her basket filled, she put it on her back & followed the indians [sic] towards the beach while we walked behind; each of us carrying some of her things. Seal meat[,] some of it stinking & a seal[']s head from which putrefied brains was [sic] running was all carefully put into the basket. wWe soon arrived at a spring of water where we stop[p]ed & on some stakes which we found standing near we hung the things we were carrying fixing them on the stakes in such a manner as to lead her to believe we took very great care of them. Near this spring there were several rocks in the cracks of which were [a] large number of fish & other bones, carefully placed. We then proceeded to the beach where a spring issues from a shelving rock, just below the bank. The old woman stopped here to wash, the men having gone on ahead & [...]Charles & I remained on the bank above. This being finished, we proceeded to the boat & went on board the vessel schr. When we put her into the boat she crept forward to the bow where she knelt holding firmly on to either side of the boat. As soon as we got on board she crept along side of the stove which was on deck. Dinner was ready & was at once served. The cook gave the old woman some pork & Hard tack, which she seemed to relish, & in fact she took readily to all of our food, it always agreeing with her. Charley Brown at once set to work & made her a petticoat of ticking, which, with a man[']s cotton shirt & a black neck tie, he completed her dress, & she seemed to be very proud of it. Seeing Charley at work on her petticoat she made signs that she wanted to sew. Accordingly she was given a needle & thread but Charley was obliged to thread it for her as her eyes seemed weak.

I had given her an old cloak or cape that was almost in ribbons & she sewed up all the rents & holes. Her manner of sewing wa[s] peculiar. Placing her work across her knee she thrust the needle through the cloth with the right hand & pulled the thread taught [taut] with the left. The next day we went ashore & camped about the middle of the Island close to the beach. We made a temporary shelter by spreading a sail over two oars driven into the side of the bank. A similar shelter was made for her of brush[.]

We remained here hunting about a month, when we brought her on shore with us. While on they [sic] Island with us she busied herself in going for wood & water about a quarter of a mile distance & working on her baskets. She brought water & wood of her own accord, the water in the vessels before mentioned.

Of the several baskets she was working not one of them was completed, although she would work first on one then on the other. One day Charley shot a she otter off shore. It was brought to land for the purpose of skinning. Inside of her was a young otter, within a few days of being born. The carcass was being haulded down to the water as was customary after taking off the skin, when the old woman vigorously protested against such a waste of meat. Seizing one of the flippers she drew it back on land, where it lay until the stench obliged us to throw it in the water. By this time however she had come to the conclusion that our food was better than this & she so expressed herself in her own rude way by signs. She was very fond of sugar & fin fact anything sweet & showed her fondness for it by smacking her lips. She had evidently known hunger as she sedulously saved every scrap of food & bones; & the latter she would take out from time to time suck them over & over & then put they [them] away again.
When we took her from her hut she was very carefull [careful] to place the seal's head in the basket although it was almost rotten.

The young otter was skinned & stuffed, making a plaything for the old woman. She hung it by a string from the roof of her shelter & for hours at a time would amuse herself like a child in making it swing back & forth, striking it with her hand to keep it in motion.

One day while out hunting I came across her lining one of the vessels she used for holding water. She had built a fire & had several small stones about the size of a walnut heating in it. Taking one of the vessels which was in shape & size very like a demijohn, excepting that the neck & mouth were much longer, she dropped a few pieces of asphaltum within it, & as soon as the stones were well heating [heated] they were dropped in on top of the asphaltum. They soon melted it when resting the bottom of the vessel on the ground she gave it a rotary motion with both hands until the its interior was completely covered with the asphaltum[.]

These vessels hold water well, & if kept full may be placed with safety in a hot sun. When we left the Island for Santa Barbara, we were caught in such a violent gale that we were several times on the point of turning back, but we finally got under the lee of Sta. Cruz Island, which afforded us some shelter until late in the day when the wind went down.

As soon as it began to blow the old woman conveyed to us by signs her intention to stop the wind. She then knelt & prayed facing the quarter fro[m] which the wind blowed, & continued to pray at intervals during the day until the gale was over. Then she looked at us & smiled as much as to say, "You see how I have succeeded in stopping the wind!["]
From Santa Cruz we ran over to Santa Barbara arriving there early the next day.

Upon nearing the shore an ox-cart came in sight when the old woman's delight was unbounded. She clapped her hands & danced pointing the while at the cart & oxen. On landing I found my sons at the beach awaiting my arrival, one of them being on horseback.

Here [Her] delight at the sight of the horse was even greater than that manifested at the sight of the ox-cart. As soon as she got out of the boat, she went up to it & began examining it, pointing at this part then that & talking & laughing to herself. Finally she pointed at the horse & placing two fingers of her right hand astride the fore finger of her left, she imitated the motion of a horse.
The news was not long in spreading of the arrival of the old woman & we had barely reached my house with her when half of the town came down to see her.
For months after[,] she & her thing[s], such as her dress, baskets, needle[s], &c., were visited by every body in the town & for miles around outside of it.
The old woman was always in good humor & sang & danced, to the great delight of the children & even old ones. She often visited the town & seldom returned without some present.

The vessels that touched here usually brought passengers who[,] hearing of her[,] came to my house. The Capt. of the "Fremont"[,] one of these vessels[,] offered to take her to San Francisco & exhibit her, giving me one half of what he could make. Capt. Trussel of this place offered me $1000 for her for the same purpose. We had all become somewhat attached to her however & consequently refused to listen to these proposals.

The same day we arrived here th[e] Fathers from the Mission came down to see her. They continued to visit her & also sent for indians [sic] from different parts of this section, & speaking different indian [Indian] tongues, in hopes of finding some one who could converse with her.

Several came, each representing a different dialect, but none of them could understand her or make themselves understood. She was continually talking & frequently made use of the pickininy [pickaninny], in referring to her child. She also used mañana[.] She expressed a great many ideas by signs so plainly that we readily understood them. By signs she told us that she did not find her child, that she wandered about for days without tasting hardly any food or drink sometimes sleeping but little, until her clothes were torn, & her feet & legs bleeding.

After a time she forgot her child & sang & danced. She also told that she was very sick at one time; that she had seen vessels passing two [to] & fro but none came to take her off; that she saw us on the Island before we found her.

Her dresses, bone needles & other curiosities where [were] taken possession of by Father Gonzal[e]s, with my consent & sent to Rome. About 4 5 weeks after she was brought over, she was taken sick from eating too much fruit & 7 weeks from the day of her arrival died. The Fathers of the Mission baptised her [...] sub conditione & named her Juana Maria. I left here for San Francisco just before she died having first made her a rough coffin. My wife can tell you better about her after I brought her ashore.
Since this date I have remained here in Santa Barbara making an occasional otter hunt.
